title: #customer-success: 3135792468
preview:
Elena Ward: Quick batch update — pulled 20 in-product survey rows (NPS followups), 14 voice snippets from onboarding, and 6 support threads. Aim: surface top 8 distinct customer asks (atomic), tag product/enablement/infra, and mark any "renewal_cohort" customers for rapid escalation. Need a tidy CSV + 1-slide summary for PM sync tomorrow.

Marcus Lin: I can run the first pass. Plan: merge telemetry (event_id) + ASR transcripts + support excerpts, run sentiment + NLU extraction, then human-validate low-confidence items. Will output one row per atomic ask.

Sophie Turner: Love the telemetry tie-in. For scoring, let's compute two scores:
- signal_score = normalized frequency * 2 + (sentiment_neg ? 3 : 0) + (explicit_request ? 2 : 0)
- churn_alert = renewal_cohort ? 1 : 0 (and escalate if signal_score >=7 || churn_alert==1)
Also add 'evidence_refs' column with event_ids/clip_ids/ticket_ids.

Jorge Alvarez: CSV header suggestion (minimal):
```
customer,source,ref_id,ask,category,signal_score,churn_alert,sentiment,enablement_ok,evidence_refs,notes
```

signal-synth-bot: Uploaded merged bundle: inprod_survey_voice_3135792468.zip -> https://drive.redwood.example/cs/bundles/inprod_survey_voice_3135792468.zip :file_folder:

Marcus Lin: Ran NLU + sentiment. Sample rows (first-pass):
NimbusHealth,inproduct,event_9921,\"Per-route cost toggle in Console\",\"billing\",8,1,negative,false,\"event_9921,clip_nim_03\",\"explicit during renewal survey\"
ParcelCo,voice,pc_clip_07,\"Clarify token billing for batch embeddings\",\"billing\",5,0,neutral,true,\"pc_clip_07,ticket_pc_402\",\"enablement could help\"
Veridian,support,vd-77,\"Private model pinning required for compliance\",\"infra\",9,1,negative,false,\"vd-77,clip_vd_01\",\"security lead requested on-call\"

Sophie Turner: Nice. For dedupe: group by fuzzy-match on 'ask' text + same pattern across sources -> produce canonical pattern_id. If cluster_size >=3 create pattern ticket for PMs with all evidence refs.

Elena Ward: Good call on pattern tickets. Also, add a 'sampling_bias' note if the ask source is mostly surveys (we don't want to overweight surveys vs support calls).

Marcus Lin: Noted — I'll include a confidence label (high/med/low) and a sampling_bias field in notes. Also flagged 2 survey responses that looked like 'noise' (one-liners) -> confidence=low.

Jorge Alvarez: Automation rules proposal:
- If signal_score >= 7 OR churn_alert==1 -> auto-open 'customer-voice-escalation' ticket and ping the CSM + account exec.
- If enablement_ok==true and signal_score <7 -> create a docs task in 'cs-enablement'.
- If pattern cluster_size >=3 -> create a pattern-card in PM backlog for grouping.

Sophie Turner: For auto-tickets include: short ask, top 2 evidence refs, sentiment excerpt, suggested owner_reco (product/enablement/infra), and estimated_effort (S/M/L).

Marcus Lin: Uploaded CSV: inprod_voice_extract_3135792468.csv -> https://drive.redwood.example/cs/exports/inprod_voice_extract_3135792468.csv. I flagged 3 low-audio clips as 'needs-listen' and 1 row as 'needs-legal' (customer quoted PII in transcript).

Elena Ward: Great. Jorge please own the PM-facing one-pager (top 5 product asks, top 3 escalations with renewal context, and pattern heatmap). Sophie will own dedupe/cluster IDs. Marcus will wire the auto-ticket preview for review.

signal-synth-bot: Reminder: CS->Product triage tomorrow 09:30. Agenda auto-added: \"In-product survey + voice harvest 3135792468: top patterns & churn alerts\".

Sophie Turner: Quick question — for 'renewal_cohort' logic, should we include customers with upcoming contract renegotiation in 90 days or only those flagged by CSMs?

Elena Ward: Include both. We tag 'renewal_cohort' if either CSM flagged or contract_end <= 90d. We should escalate if churn_alert==1 even if signal_score is marginal.

Marcus Lin: Understood. I'll push final CSV with pattern_id column and the auto-ticket preview before EOD.

Jorge Alvarez: Thanks team. If anyone needs access to the bundle, DM me. See you in the triage. :thumbsup:

title: #all-hands: 1978001234
preview:
Maya Chen: Thanks everyone for joining the all-hands — quick follow-up thread for the Q&A items I flagged. I'll paste the highlights and we can assign owners.

Tom Alvarez: thanks Maya. can you drop the three top asks you want tracked here?

Maya Chen: 1) Clarify Dedicated rollout timeline for Q3 (commit months, beta vs GA). 2) Action items for the Reliability SLAs we promised. 3) Next steps on the pricing experiment for burst capacity.

Rachel Singh: Re: Dedicated timeline — we still expect beta in late July and GA in mid-September if no infra blockers. I'll put a date matrix in the roadmap doc. link: https://redwood.internal/roadmap/dedicated-q3 (wip)

Tom Alvarez: timeline ack. @leo can infra confirm any critical blockers that would shift that?

Leo Park: infra: main risk is the autoscaler work and a small kernel perf regression on 8-bit quant on one card type. engineering plan: finish autoscaler by 7/10, perf fix by 7/20. if both done, timeline stands.

Maya Chen: can you add a quick risk flag and owner to the roadmap doc so PMs can see it?

Leo Park: will do. owner: Leo Park. ETA: 2026-07-05 for the doc entry.

Rachel Singh: Action: I'll move beta date to 2026-08-01 in the public-facing calendar but mark internal GA goal 2026-09-15. Owner: Rachel.

Marcus Lin: From Support's side — need a runbook and known-failure list for Dedicated beta. Can Eng supply a first pass?

Tom Alvarez: Eng will provide. Owner: Tom (coord) + Leo (infra) + SRE. Deliverable: initial runbook + playbook. Due: 2026-07-25.

Maya Chen: SLA asks: leadership committed to a 99.9% monthly uptime for Dedicated customers and a latency SLO for 95th p50 targets. We need a clear ops + billing mapping for credits if we miss.

Anita Gomez: ops/comms note — we'll need statement templates for customers and a billing policy draft. I'll own the customer comms template. ETA: 2026-07-30.

Tom Alvarez: Billing policy owner: Finance + Tom. I'll loop in Nisha from finance after this thread.

Maya Chen: Pricing experiment notes: we said we'd trial burst capacity pricing with 3 customers, run a 6-week pilot, and share results. Need owners and a measurement plan.

Rachel Singh: I'll own pilot design and measurement. Owner: Rachel. Pilot plan doc: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PilotBurst (private) — will share access. KPIs: revenue per token, avg latency under burst, failover rate.

Marcus Lin: support readiness: we need playbooks for burst throttling and overflow policies. Will review once pilot doc is shared.

Tom Alvarez: I'll set up a weekly 30-min sync for the next 6 weeks starting 2026-06-10 to track progress on timeline + runbook + pilot. Calendar invite to follow.

Maya Chen: thanks. quick wrap: I'll synthesize this thread into three action items and owners: 1) Dedicated timeline + risk flags — Rachel (PM) + Leo (owner) — due 2026-07-05 for internal doc. 2) Runbook + SLA mapping + billing policy — Tom + Leo + Finance + Anita — due 2026-07-30. 3) Burst pricing pilot design + measurement — Rachel — pilot to start 2026-07-20.

deploy-bot: Deployment v2.18.4 to canary succeeded (commit ef3a1d) — infra autoscaler tests passed in ci/canary.

Leo Park: noted — the canary pass reduces timeline risk. I'll update the roadmap doc now.

Maya Chen: perfect. If anyone needs me to own any of the write-ups, ping me. thanks all :thumbsup: 

Rachel Singh: will follow up with the shared docs + invite.

Marcus Lin: adding Support checklist to the pilot doc by 2026-07-10.

Anita Gomez: I'll draft the customer comms template and post here for review by 2026-07-25.

Tom Alvarez: closing loop — calendar invite + initial runbook template in #eng-runbooks by EOD today.

Maya Chen: great, thread closed unless someone has pressing follow-ups.

title: #docs: 1769599011
preview:
Elliot Price: hey #docs — quick PR review request. We’ve got the enterprise security page updates ready to merge and I want eyes from Security/GRC + messaging before we ship.

Links:
- PR 2841 (enterprise security overview + gated SOC 2 request link stub): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-docs/pull/2841
- PR 2849 (audit logging + export options): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-docs/pull/2849
- PR 2860 (clarify data retention + residency language + glossary): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-docs/pull/2860

Main asks:
1) confirm we’re not over-claiming anything (SOC2 / ISO / pen test wording)
2) confirm evidence request flow link + language is correct (gated, NDA)
3) sanity check Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private distinctions

If you can, please leave comments directly in the PRs by EOD tomorrow. :pray:

Kira Thompson: taking first pass on 2860 now. The glossary is super helpful.

Wei Chen: I’ll review 2841 + link behavior. Also will run the docs link checker locally.

Naomi Feldman: I can do a product-security alignment pass. Please flag any “roadmap-ish” language you think is borderline.

Marcus Lin: I’ll review security claims and IR/vuln mgmt phrasing. Can you point me to the specific sections where we mention pen tests / scanning?

Elliot Price: @Marcus Lin sure — in 2841 it’s under “Security program” + FAQ accordion. In 2849 there’s a short note about “continuous monitoring” that might need tightening.

redwood-docs-bot: CI checks started for PR #2860 (build, lint, link-check).

Daniel Carter: jumping on 2841. For SOC 2, we need to be careful about “certified” language. We can say “independently audited” and “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” (assuming that’s still accurate). I’ll comment in-line.

Camila Reyes: I’ll do messaging sweep (tone + competitive-safe). Heads up: avoid “bank-grade” / “military-grade” style claims.

Talia Benson: following — this is part of the kit refresh. Once merged, I’ll link these pages from the enablement kit index.

Wei Chen: link check update: the “Request SOC 2 report” button in 2841 currently points to `/trust/soc2` which 404s on preview.

Wei Chen: suggestion: either point to the evidence request playbook page (Confluence internal) for logged-in users, OR make the docs link go to a public “Contact sales / request trust docs” page and keep the actual SOC2 report gated behind the form.

Elliot Price: good catch. We don’t want public docs pointing to internal Confluence.

Elliot Price: I’ll swap it to the public intake route (we have the security@ alias + sales form). We can keep the exact evidence process in Confluence.

Kira Thompson: on 2860: one nit — the sentence “Redwood supports EU data residency” reads too absolute. The rest of the doc does a good job explaining regional availability and that some metadata may be processed outside the region depending on deployment mode.

Kira Thompson: maybe: “Redwood offers region selection in supported cloud regions. For EU residency requirements, Redwood Dedicated and Redwood Private can be deployed in EU regions; please confirm regional availability with your account team.”

Naomi Feldman: +1. Also for Hosted, we should avoid implying we can guarantee residency for *all* processing unless we truly can. Recommend anchoring it to “in-scope services” + “customer-selected region” and push strict residency to Dedicated/Private.

Daniel Carter: left comments on 2841 re: SOC 2 language. Proposed replacement:
- “Redwood maintains a SOC 2 Type II report covering the Redwood Inference platform. The report is available to customers and prospects under NDA upon request.”
Also please remove “SOC 2 certified” (not a thing).

Marcus Lin: reviewed 2841 + 2849.
- 2841: change “regular penetration testing” to “periodic penetration testing” unless we’re stating frequency. Questionnaires often ask for annual vs quarterly.
- 2849: the phrase “all admin actions are logged” is too broad. We should say “console and API administrative events are captured in audit logs” and refer to the list of event types.

Marcus Lin: also: avoid “real-time alerting” unless we ship alerting as a product feature. Export-to-SIEM is ok; alerts are on customer side.

Camila Reyes: did a sweep. Biggest thing: in 2841 there’s a line “we never retain prompts.” That’s not consistent with the retention language doc (we do have default retention windows + customer-configurable options in certain modes). Suggest changing to:
“By default, Redwood limits retention of customer content. Retention and logging behavior varies by deployment mode and configuration. See Data retention & deletion for details.”

Elliot Price: thank you. Updating now.

redwood-docs-bot: CI checks passed for PR #2860.

Elliot Price: pushed fixes:
- PR 2841: updated SOC 2 wording per @Daniel Carter, removed “certified,” changed pen-test language to “periodic,” updated “request trust docs” link to public form.
- PR 2849: tightened audit log language per @Marcus Lin (“captured events” vs “all actions”), removed “real-time alerting” phrasing.
- PR 2860: softened EU residency statement + made Hosted vs Dedicated/Private distinctions clearer.

Elliot Price: can folks re-check those sections and drop an approval if good?

Daniel Carter: re-checked 2841. SOC2 language looks good now. Approved. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: re-checked 2849 + 2841 changes. Looks aligned. Approved.

Naomi Feldman: skimmed final diff across all three. No roadmap promises. Looks consistent with product behavior as described. Approved.

Wei Chen: ran link checker locally — no broken links now. The “request trust docs” form resolves. Approved.

Kira Thompson: reviewed the updated residency/retention wording in 2860. This reads much safer + clearer. Approved.

Elliot Price: awesome, thanks all. Merging 2860 today, then 2849 + 2841 right after. Next step is updating the enablement kit index to point to these pages + adding release note in the FAQ changelog.

Talia Benson: :thumbsup: once merged, please send me the final URLs so we can update the kit tracker + HubSpot snippets.

Elliot Price: will do.

title: #docs: 1769599015
preview:
Elliot Price: need quick alignment on marketplace onboarding docs structure/IA before we lock URLs for AWS/GCP submission forms. where do we want the nav entry + where do we link security + troubleshooting?

Alex Martinez: +1. partner portals want stable URLs and we keep getting asked “link to onboarding steps” + “link to security overview”. if we change paths post-submit it’s a mess.

Wei Chen: current draft is kind of scattered (Hosted quickstart vs marketplace quickstart vs pricing pages). i’d vote for a single “Marketplace” landing page with the flow, then split by AWS/GCP.

Kira Thompson: agree. also we need to handle Dedicated/Private buyers who come in via marketplace (not just Hosted). landing page should branch by “What you bought” (Hosted usage-based vs Dedicated capacity vs Private deployment).

Ben Carter: from funnel POV, keep step count low on landing page. the most common drop is “i subscribed… now what?” so above the fold should be 3–5 steps max, then expand.

Elliot Price: proposed IA (rough):
- Getting started
  - Marketplace (landing)
    - AWS Marketplace onboarding
    - GCP Marketplace onboarding
    - Troubleshooting (entitlements/provisioning/billing)
- Security
  - Marketplace security overview (public)
  - Data handling & retention (public-ish)
  - Audit logs (public-ish)
  - Evidence (gated / on request)

Thoughts?

Naomi Feldman: security pages: we can publish a “marketplace security overview” + shared responsibility model + high-level encryption/keys. BUT evidence links should NOT be public. we can point to “SOC 2 Type II available under NDA / via trust portal” wording.

Marcus Lin: +1. also be careful with “supports” vs “enforces” language on retention (configurable vs default). pls link to the retention doc but keep it explicit what’s default and what’s configurable.

Tessa Morgan: UX note: landing page should visually mirror console screens (Subscribe → Verify entitlement → Create/link org → Create API key → First request). even if docs are text-heavy, a simple diagram helps.

Jean-Paul Laurent: if we put “Marketplace” under Getting started, can we also add a callout on the generic “Quickstart” pages that says “Bought via AWS/GCP marketplace? start here” w/ link? otherwise people will follow old quickstart and then get confused.

Alex Martinez: yes, we should do a small “marketplace entrypoint” banner on quickstarts + on pricing page.

Wei Chen: for Hosted path, AWS/GCP guides should share 80% of the same steps. can we avoid duplicate content by having one “marketplace flow” page and then AWS/GCP pages that are mostly provider-specific bits (subscription screenshot + entitlement timing + where to find IDs)?

Elliot Price: we can do:
- /marketplace (overview + common flow)
- /marketplace/aws (AWS-specific: subscribe UI, customer identifier, private offer activation, entitlement propagation notes)
- /marketplace/gcp (GCP-specific: procurement steps, billing account selection, etc)
- /marketplace/troubleshooting (shared)

Kira Thompson: pls include “what plan did I buy?” section somewhere. example: Dedicated buyers need to know they still need to talk to SE to provision capacity / region + that marketplace subscription is the procurement layer, not the infra magically appearing.

Ben Carter: +1 but keep it crisp. maybe a “Choose your deployment” section with 3 tiles: Hosted / Dedicated / Private. each tile shows: what you get immediately vs what requires coordination.

Naomi Feldman: also link security overview from the landing page, but don’t make onboarding block on it. procurement folks will go hunting though—so prominent placement is good.

Marcus Lin: troubleshooting page should include entitlement timing expectations (e.g., “can take up to X minutes”), retry guidance, and what info to include in a support ticket (org id, marketplace provider, subscription id, region). keep it practical.

Alex Martinez: we should standardize the URLs we share with partners:
1) onboarding landing
2) security overview
3) troubleshooting
That’s what AWS asked for last time.

Elliot Price: what do we call the nav item? “Marketplace onboarding” vs just “Marketplace”? (trying not to bury it)

Wei Chen: nav label “Marketplace” is fine. H1 on landing can be “Marketplace onboarding (AWS & GCP)”.

Tessa Morgan: agree. “Marketplace” in nav is easy to scan.

Kira Thompson: for troubleshooting, can we split sections by symptom?
- I subscribed but Redwood says no entitlement
- I can’t link to an existing org
- I created an org but no API key page / permissions
- Billing dimension mismatch / unexpected line items

Ben Carter: yes please. and add “still stuck? contact support” with clear routing. marketplace customers always ask “AWS or Redwood?”

Naomi Feldman: + make sure support routing doesn’t imply AWS has access to our systems. “Contact Redwood support; AWS/GCP can only help with marketplace purchase mechanics.”

Marcus Lin: also add an “Incident/status” link if we have one. entitlement sync job issues will look like user error.

Alex Martinez: URLs / redirects: we have an internal request in flight for vanity URLs. i’d like to keep these stable:
- /docs/marketplace
- /docs/marketplace/aws
- /docs/marketplace/gcp
- /docs/marketplace/troubleshooting
- /docs/security/marketplace
If we need legacy redirects from old marketplace pages, now’s the time.

Elliot Price: ok. action items:
- Elliot: update IA in docs repo + nav + stubs with final URLs (today)
- Wei/Kira: confirm content owners per page (Hosted bits vs Dedicated/Private bits) (tomorrow)
- Naomi/Marcus: review wording for security links + retention/audit log claims (this week)
- Alex: coordinate redirects/vanity URL ticket + confirm with Partnerships which exact links go into AWS/GCP submission forms

Ben Carter: pls add a short “What happens after you subscribe?” section that includes entitlement delay expectations + a link to troubleshooting. reduces support load.

Elliot Price: :thumbsup: will do. I’ll post the updated sitemap once the PR is up.

Alex Martinez: thanks. once PR is live, i’ll share with Partnerships + PM for sign-off.

Marcus Lin: ping me on the PR. I want to double check we’re not over-promising on retention defaults.

Naomi Feldman: same. also let’s keep evidence references as “available upon request” and link to the security overview page rather than a dead-end.

Wei Chen: cool. i’ll start consolidating the common flow content so AWS/GCP pages are genuinely provider-specific.

Kira Thompson: and I’ll add the Dedicated/Private callouts + “what you get immediately” section.

Elliot Price: perfect. closing loop: nav = Getting started → Marketplace; Security section includes Marketplace security overview; troubleshooting is a first-class page under Marketplace. done.

title: #docs: 1769395510
preview:
Elliot Price: Quick q for @Naomi Feldman / @Dr. Aisha Rahman / @Olga Petrov — for the marketplace security packet, what is OK to publish publicly on docs vs what needs to be gated (sales-assisted / NDA / on-request)? We need to lock this today so we can finalize IA + URLs for AWS/GCP forms.

Alex Martinez: +1. AWS asked for “security overview” link that a reviewer can open without emailing us. GCP similar. But we don’t want to accidentally publish anything we normally keep in Trust Center.

Naomi Feldman: Can we list the specific sections you’re thinking about? Some content is fine public (high-level), some should definitely be gated (SOC2 report, pen test).

Elliot Price: Draft sections (from the Confluence index):
1) Security overview + shared responsibility
2) Data flow diagram
3) Encryption (in transit/at rest), KMS options
4) Audit logs (what events, export)
5) Data retention/residency
6) Subprocessors
7) SOC2 / ISO posture + evidence pointers
8) Vulnerability mgmt / pen tests

Wei Chen: Also there’s copy in the listing that currently says “SOC2 + ISO compliant” (from an older block). I’m worried that’s not precise.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Great flag. We should avoid “ISO compliant” unless we’re certified and can substantiate. Use “SOC 2 Type II” if accurate, and for ISO say “ISO 27001-aligned controls” or “ISO 27001 program in progress” (whatever is true).

Olga Petrov: For clarity: we have SOC 2 Type II report for the current period (available under NDA). We are not publicly posting the full report. ISO 27001: we’re aligned and in audit prep, but not certified yet.

Marcus Lin: +1 on gating anything that reveals control implementation details (e.g., specific tools/vendors in depth, network diagrams with too much detail, test results).

Kira Thompson: What about the data flow diagram? The draft is pretty high-level (Request -> Gateway -> Router -> Inference Runtime -> Logging/Metrics). No IPs/regions listed.

Naomi Feldman: High-level data flow is fine public if it’s abstract (boxes + arrows) and doesn’t enumerate internal service names, cloud account IDs, or exact infra topology.

Alex Martinez: AWS marketplace review explicitly asked for “architecture diagram + data flow” in the call notes. If we gate that, it’ll slow approval.

Sofia Mendes: Looping in quickly — from legal perspective: public is OK for general security statements if they are accurate, non-misleading, and don’t create contractual commitments. Avoid absolutes like “always,” “guaranteed,” “zero access.” Prefer “designed to,” “we implement,” “by default,” “customers can configure.”

Camila Reyes: Marketing side: we also don’t want a doc that reads like a compliance report. Needs to be buyer-friendly. But agreed, we should provide something linkable for marketplace.

Elliot Price: Proposed split:
PUBLIC docs page: Security Overview for Marketplace (includes 1-6 at high level)
GATED/On-request: SOC2 report, bridge letter, pen test summary, vuln scan outputs, detailed policies
Does that align?

Olga Petrov: Yes. Also: subprocessor list can be public, but keep it to the standard “Subprocessors” page we already maintain (names + purpose + location). Don’t include internal DPAs or MSAs.

Wei Chen: For SOC2/ISO section on the public page, can we say:
- “Redwood maintains a SOC 2 Type II report (available under NDA upon request).”
- “Redwood’s security program is aligned to ISO 27001 control domains.”
And link to “Request security documents” form.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That wording is acceptable. Add timeframe language carefully (e.g., “current SOC 2 Type II report”) but don’t mention audit firm name publicly.

Marcus Lin: On encryption: OK to state “TLS 1.2+ in transit” and “AES-256 at rest” if that’s accurate across all storage types (object store, DB, logs). If there are exceptions, qualify.

Naomi Feldman: Please avoid enumerating exact cipher suites. Keep it “industry-standard encryption in transit (TLS)” and “encryption at rest” + “customer-managed keys available for Private / Dedicated (where applicable).” We should be explicit which deployment modes support CMK.

Kira Thompson: For retention/residency: we can publish defaults + what’s configurable (e.g., request logs retention window, prompt/response logging toggles), but avoid stating exact numbers that might change without review.

Alex Martinez: Our retention doc currently has exact defaults (e.g., “X days”). That’s been useful for procurement.

Sofia Mendes: If you publish exact retention durations, treat them as product promises. If we do, we need a change management process + review when defaults change.

Olga Petrov: Suggest: public page says “By default, metadata and operational logs are retained for a limited period to support reliability and abuse prevention” + “customers can configure retention in Dedicated/Private; Hosted has fixed defaults.” Then have a gated appendix with exact numbers (or in the Trust Center).

Elliot Price: That’s doable. But marketplace reviewers sometimes want the actual number.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Compromise: put exact numbers in a “Marketplace Security Addendum (Customer-Facing)” PDF that is accessible after lightweight gating (e.g., email capture + click-through) OR “available to verified marketplace reviewers upon request.” AWS reviewers will email if they need it. We should not post sensitive retention specifics without a control process.

Alex Martinez: For onboarding friction: if AWS wants a URL in the submission, we can put the public overview URL + a “request docs” URL. That might pass.

Marcus Lin: Audit logs section: OK to list event categories (org changes, API key events, SSO config, model deployment changes, admin actions) and export destinations (S3, GCS, SIEM via webhook) without sharing schemas.

Wei Chen: Also, do we include “We don’t train on customer data” on the public page? That’s asked constantly.

Sofia Mendes: Be careful: depends on definitions. Safer: “Customer prompts and outputs are not used to train Redwood-hosted foundation models by default.” If there are exceptions (explicit opt-in), state that.

Naomi Feldman: +1. And clarify: logs may be used for abuse detection and service improvement (aggregated/limited), but not for model training absent opt-in.

Camila Reyes: Please avoid heavy hedging that reads like “we might do anything.” Buyers want clarity. Maybe: “We do not use customer content to train models unless the customer explicitly opts in.”

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That’s fine if it’s accurate. Also note that for Private deployments, customer controls the environment and can enforce stricter controls.

Elliot Price: Action items:
- @Elliot Price to draft PUBLIC “Marketplace Security Overview” page with: shared responsibility, high-level data flow, encryption, audit logs, subprocessors link, high-level retention/residency, and a “Request security docs” CTA.
- @Naomi Feldman / @Marcus Lin to review encryption + audit logs wording for accuracy.
- @Olga Petrov to provide approved SOC2/ISO posture language + what we can/can’t say publicly.
- @Sofia Mendes to review for legal sensitivity + avoid contractual commitments.

Alex Martinez: Can we also align on a single sentence for marketplace listing copy re: compliance? We keep getting comments back from AWS on over-claiming.

Olga Petrov: Suggested: “Redwood maintains a SOC 2 Type II report and follows a risk-based security program aligned to ISO 27001 domains. Compliance evidence is available upon request.”

Wei Chen: :thumbsup: I’ll update the copy block to that.

Marcus Lin: One more: don’t publish the full incident response policy publicly. But we can say we have 24/7 monitoring + documented IR process.

Naomi Feldman: Agree. Also no details on red team frequency, external vendor names, or exact SLA response times unless those are in contracts.

Elliot Price: Got it. I’ll post a draft link in this thread tomorrow for review.

Camila Reyes: Thanks. Once we have the public page, we’ll use it for the marketplace listing “Security” section and for procurement emails.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please add a small “Last updated” line and an email for reporting vulnerabilities (security@redwoodinference.com) on the public page.

Elliot Price: Will do.

title: #announcements: 1768675500
preview:
Connor O'Brien: :mega: SDK parity update is shipping today: standardized **auth + retries/backoff + timeouts + streaming semantics** across Python / TypeScript / Go.

Why: we’ve had too many “works in TS but not in Python” issues around 429s, timeouts, and stream disconnect behavior. This release aligns defaults + configuration knobs, and makes errors / request_id surfaced consistently.

✅ Released versions
• Python: `redwood-sdk` **v1.9.0**
• TypeScript: `@redwood-ai/sdk` **v2.4.0**
• Go: `github.com/redwood-ai/redwood-go` **v1.6.0**

What changed (high level)
1) Auth resolution (now consistent)
• Precedence: explicit client param > env var(s) > (legacy env vars w/ deprecation warning)
• Env vars (preferred): `REDWOOD_API_KEY` (API key) or `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (bearer)
• Legacy names still work for now but will warn in logs (no secret values printed).

2) Retry policy (now consistent)
• Retries enabled by default for **429 + 5xx + transient network errors**
• Honors `Retry-After` (seconds + HTTP-date formats)
• Exponential backoff w/ jitter, capped, and bounded max attempts (defaults tuned to avoid thundering herd)
• Typed errors now include `request_id`, `status_code`, and `retryable` where available.

3) Timeouts (now consistent)
• Unified model: connect vs read vs overall timeout (mapped per language client)
• Defaults adjusted to be production-safe; streaming has distinct behavior (see below)

4) Streaming semantics (now consistent)
• If a stream disconnects mid-flight, SDKs surface a typed streaming disconnect error and include `request_id` when present.
• SDKs do **not** automatically retry once any partial output has been received (to avoid duplicate side effects / billing confusion). Docs include safe retry guidance + idempotency notes.

Docs / migration notes
• Migration guide (config changes, deprecated env vars, examples): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGDX/sdk-migration-notes-auth-retry-timeout
• Standard (retry/timeout details): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/sdk-retry-timeout-standard
• Streaming quickstart (disconnect handling + timeouts): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGDX/streaming-quickstart-consistent-behavior

Rollout notes
• These are intended to be low-breakage changes. Where behavior may differ materially (esp. timeouts), the migration guide calls it out + shows how to restore old behavior.
• Please route any “this changed my production behavior” reports to Ibrahim + the language owner with SDK version + `request_id` if you have it.

Big thanks to Monica / Ibrahim / Sabrina / Dana / Mateusz + SRE + Security + Docs for getting this over the line. :tada:

Monica Patel: Thanks everyone. This is the first release gated by the cross-language conformance suite, so we should see fewer regressions going forward.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. If you hit anything weird, please include:
- SDK + version
- whether it was streaming vs non-streaming
- the `request_id` (now included on most raised errors)
- and if you were behind a proxy / corp network (re: stream disconnects)

Sabrina James: Python install/upgrade:
`pip install -U redwood-sdk`
Config options are in the migration doc, but tl;dr `timeout`, `max_retries`, and `retry_backoff` are now the same names/semantics as TS/Go.

Dana Lo: TS upgrade:
`npm i @redwood-ai/sdk@^2.4.0`
Note: we kept the older env var aliasing but you’ll see a one-time deprecation warning on startup if you’re using the old names.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go upgrade:
`go get github.com/redwood-ai/redwood-go@v1.6.0`
Context cancellation now reliably aborts both HTTP and streaming reads (we had some gaps there before).

Allison Grant: SRE note: during rollout please keep an eye on retry rate + 429s; defaults are designed to be conservative, but if you see spiky behavior in a service, ping #eng-sre. Dashboards are live with dimensions for `retry_count`, `timeout_ms`, and streaming disconnect reasons.

Marcus Lin: Security reminder: please do *not* log raw env vars or headers. The SDKs redact secrets by default; if you’ve wrapped clients with your own debug logging, make sure you’re not dumping `Authorization`.

Wei Chen: Docs updates are live as of this morning; if you find an example that still uses old env var names, drop a note in #docs and we’ll patch quickly.

Elliot Price: Also, if you maintain internal quickstarts/templates, please update them to the new env var names + consistent timeout knobs. We saw a few internal repos copying older snippets.

- redwood-sdk 1.9.0 (PyPI)
- @redwood-ai/sdk 2.4.0 (npm)
- redwood-go v1.6.0 (git tag)

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: We’ll post a follow-up in ~1 week with early impact (support tickets, retry/timeout metrics, and any hotfixes).
text:
Connor O'Brien: :mega: SDK parity update is shipping today: standardized **auth + retries/backoff + timeouts + streaming semantics** across Python / TypeScript / Go.

Why: we’ve had too many “works in TS but not in Python” issues around 429s, timeouts, and stream disconnect behavior. This release aligns defaults + configuration knobs, and makes errors / request_id surfaced consistently.

✅ Released versions
• Python: `redwood-sdk` **v1.9.0**
• TypeScript: `@redwood-ai/sdk` **v2.4.0**
• Go: `github.com/redwood-ai/redwood-go` **v1.6.0**

What changed (high level)
1) Auth resolution (now consistent)
• Precedence: explicit client param > env var(s) > (legacy env vars w/ deprecation warning)
• Env vars (preferred): `REDWOOD_API_KEY` (API key) or `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (bearer)
• Legacy names still work for now but will warn in logs (no secret values printed).

2) Retry policy (now consistent)
• Retries enabled by default for **429 + 5xx + transient network errors**
• Honors `Retry-After` (seconds + HTTP-date formats)
• Exponential backoff w/ jitter, capped, and bounded max attempts (defaults tuned to avoid thundering herd)
• Typed errors now include `request_id`, `status_code`, and `retryable` where available.

3) Timeouts (now consistent)
• Unified model: connect vs read vs overall timeout (mapped per language client)
• Defaults adjusted to be production-safe; streaming has distinct behavior (see below)

4) Streaming semantics (now consistent)
• If a stream disconnects mid-flight, SDKs surface a typed streaming disconnect error and include `request_id` when present.
• SDKs do **not** automatically retry once any partial output has been received (to avoid duplicate side effects / billing confusion). Docs include safe retry guidance + idempotency notes.

Docs / migration notes
• Migration guide (config changes, deprecated env vars, examples): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGDX/sdk-migration-notes-auth-retry-timeout
• Standard (retry/timeout details): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/sdk-retry-timeout-standard
• Streaming quickstart (disconnect handling + timeouts): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGDX/streaming-quickstart-consistent-behavior

Rollout notes
• These are intended to be low-breakage changes. Where behavior may differ materially (esp. timeouts), the migration guide calls it out + shows how to restore old behavior.
• Please route any “this changed my production behavior” reports to Ibrahim + the language owner with SDK version + `request_id` if you have it.

Big thanks to Monica / Ibrahim / Sabrina / Dana / Mateusz + SRE + Security + Docs for getting this over the line. :tada:

Monica Patel: Thanks everyone. This is the first release gated by the cross-language conformance suite, so we should see fewer regressions going forward.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. If you hit anything weird, please include:
- SDK + version
- whether it was streaming vs non-streaming
- the `request_id` (now included on most raised errors)
- and if you were behind a proxy / corp network (re: stream disconnects)

Sabrina James: Python install/upgrade:
`pip install -U redwood-sdk`
Config options are in the migration doc, but tl;dr `timeout`, `max_retries`, and `retry_backoff` are now the same names/semantics as TS/Go.

Dana Lo: TS upgrade:
`npm i @redwood-ai/sdk@^2.4.0`
Note: we kept the older env var aliasing but you’ll see a one-time deprecation warning on startup if you’re using the old names.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go upgrade:
`go get github.com/redwood-ai/redwood-go@v1.6.0`
Context cancellation now reliably aborts both HTTP and streaming reads (we had some gaps there before).

Allison Grant: SRE note: during rollout please keep an eye on retry rate + 429s; defaults are designed to be conservative, but if you see spiky behavior in a service, ping #eng-sre. Dashboards are live with dimensions for `retry_count`, `timeout_ms`, and streaming disconnect reasons.

Marcus Lin: Security reminder: please do *not* log raw env vars or headers. The SDKs redact secrets by default; if you’ve wrapped clients with your own debug logging, make sure you’re not dumping `Authorization`.

Wei Chen: Docs updates are live as of this morning; if you find an example that still uses old env var names, drop a note in #docs and we’ll patch quickly.

Elliot Price: Also, if you maintain internal quickstarts/templates, please update them to the new env var names + consistent timeout knobs. We saw a few internal repos copying older snippets.

- redwood-sdk 1.9.0 (PyPI)
- @redwood-ai/sdk 2.4.0 (npm)

title: #devex: 1768249973
preview:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a quick thread on migration plan. We’re about to merge the standardized retry/timeout/auth + streaming semantics. Question: do we gate new defaults behind a feature flag (env var / client option) or ship as minor version defaults w/ deprecation warnings?

Monica Patel: +1. We need a call today because release train is getting tight. My instinct: avoid breaking changes, but also avoid “three different behaviors forever”.

Dana Lo: TS perspective: we can ship as minor if old behavior remains accessible + we don’t change signatures. But some changes *are* behavior-breaking (e.g., retrying fewer things, timeouts being enforced more aggressively).

Sabrina James: Python: same. Biggest risk is people relying on “infinite-ish” timeouts in streaming (aka hung connections that never error). The new timeouts will surface errors earlier. That’s good but could surprise.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: context cancellation semantics are also a change. Some customers currently pass context.Background() and rely on default client timeouts. If we set an overall timeout by default, they may see timeouts they didn’t previously.

Allison Grant: SRE hat: I strongly prefer we make the new retry defaults the default sooner rather than later. If we gate it behind a flag, most clients won’t opt in, and we’ll keep seeing retry storms / non-standards in the wild. But I’m also worried about surprise load if everyone flips at once.

Henry Cho: PM: customer expectations: “minor version shouldn’t break me”. If a customer upgrades and suddenly gets timeouts they never got before, Support will call it breaking, regardless of semver purity.

Ibrahim Khan: The changes we’re talking about:
- Auth env var precedence alignment + deprecations for old names
- Retry policy: only 408/409/429/5xx + network errors, honor Retry-After, exponential backoff w/ jitter, max attempts default
- Timeout model: overall + connect/read mapping per language
- Streaming: typed errors on disconnect, partial output semantics, no automatic replay unless user opts in

Monica Patel: Let’s separate “safe defaults” from “potentially breaking”. Can we ship deprecations in minor, and gate the potentially breaking runtime behavior behind an opt-in?

Dana Lo: For TS we can do something like:
- Default stays current in vX.Y
- Add `behavior: "standard" | "legacy"` (default legacy for one release)
- Print console warn once if legacy
- Flip default to standard in next minor or next major
But that still means two code paths.

Sabrina James: Python could do similar with `client = Redwood(timeout=..., retries=..., _behavior="standard")` or env var `REDWOOD_SDK_BEHAVIOR=standard`. But I really dislike leading underscore config.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go doesn’t love “behavior strings”, but could do `redwood.WithStandardDefaults()` option. Still effectively a feature flag.

Marcus Lin: Security: pls do not use env vars that encourage people to set secrets and behavior in the same place without clear names. Also don’t log env var values. If we add a behavior env var, keep it non-sensitive (obv) and make sure debug logs don’t dump the entire environment.

Allison Grant: Another angle: default retry behavior impacts platform load. If we keep legacy defaults for too long, we don’t get the client-side shaping we want (Retry-After respect + jitter). If we flip defaults immediately, customers may see fewer retries and think “Redwood is less reliable” because their app now sees 429/5xx sooner.

Henry Cho: Exactly. Also: TS had the bug where it retried 400 in some edge path (SUP-29438). Fixing that should be default ASAP, but it’s arguably a bug fix (ok for minor).

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed compromise:
1) Ship bug-fix changes as default in next minor (e.g., never retry 4xx other than 408/409/429).
2) For “timeout tightening” and “streaming disconnect typed errors”, ship as opt-in standard mode for one minor release.
3) Flip default to standard mode in following minor with loud release notes + migration guide.

Monica Patel: That seems reasonable. Timeline-wise we have ~8 weeks total, we’re midstream. If we do “opt-in for one minor”, we need to commit to flipping in the next.

Dana Lo: Concern: TS ecosystem expects minors to be safe. Flipping defaults in a minor later might still be seen as “why did it change”. But if we communicate deprecations + warn on legacy mode, it’s less surprising.

Wei Chen: Docs: if we do dual mode, docs need to show BOTH? Or just “standard mode” and add a callout “if upgrading and need legacy behavior temporarily, set X”. I’d prefer the latter to avoid docs bloat.

Sabrina James: +1: docs should primarily reflect the future state. We can add a short “Legacy behavior” section in migration notes.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, the legacy mode concept is weird because options are explicit. But we do have defaults. I can implement: default remains legacy for one release, add `WithStandardRetryPolicy()` and `WithStandardTimeouts()` options. Then in next release flip defaults.

Allison Grant: If we’re keeping legacy default for one release, can we at least add structured logging fields everywhere now? request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms, behavior_mode. That helps us see who’s using what.

Ibrahim Khan: Yes. That’s in ENG-5129 anyway. We can make behavior_mode emitted always.

Marcus Lin: Please also ensure: no API keys in logs, and error messages don’t echo headers. If we add warnings, redact any token-like strings.

Connor O'Brien: Release coordination: I need a concrete plan with version numbers. Something like:
- Python vA.B: add standard mode opt-in + legacy warnings, bug fixes always-on
- Python vA.(B+1): standard becomes default, legacy still accessible with explicit opt-out for 1 cycle
Same for TS/Go.

Monica Patel: Let’s decide on the mechanism naming so it’s consistent across languages. Not exact same API surface, but at least same concept and docs term.

Dana Lo: Suggest term: "standard" vs "legacy". (Avoid "v2" because people will think API v2.)

Sabrina James: Agree. Also avoid “strict” (sounds scary). “standard” is fine.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, we can call it “StandardDefaults” in option names.

Henry Cho: From customer comms: we should frame it as “improved reliability + predictability” and “more consistent timeouts”. Migration guide should have a “If you see new timeouts…” section.

Allison Grant: And a “If you’re getting 429s now” section with how to tune max attempts and respect Retry-After.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok, action items:
- I’ll update the migration plan doc to include 2-step rollout (opt-in standard for one minor, then flip default).
- Dana/Sabrina/Mateusz: implement `standard`/`legacy` mode switches in your SDKs (or equivalent) + warn when legacy.
- Connor: propose version targets + release sequencing.
- Wei: docs should assume standard behavior; include migration callout for temporary legacy mode.
- Marcus: quick review of warning text + env var name if we add one.

Monica Patel: Also: let’s avoid env var if possible; prefer explicit client config. But we may still want env var for CI/testing to force standard mode for conformance runs.

Dana Lo: For TS conformance tests, env var is super convenient. But we can scope it to tests only? (not documented)

Marcus Lin: If you ship an env var in code, assume it will be discovered. That’s ok, just treat it as supported and document it. Don’t make “secret” switches.

Ibrahim Khan: Got it. We’ll document it as an alternative for fleet-wide config, but recommend code-based config.

Allison Grant: Please make sure legacy warnings are rate-limited (warn once). Don’t spam logs.

Sabrina James: Python: I can do a once-per-process warning. TS similar.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: we can log via injected logger; default no-op. We can add a single warning when constructing client.

Monica Patel: Decision captured: 2-phase rollout with standard mode opt-in first, then default flip, with clear migration notes. :thumbsup:

Ibrahim Khan: I’ll summarize in ENG-5120 and link this thread. Thanks all.

title: #eng-oncall: 1768161022
preview:
Owen Phillips: Heads up, we keep bouncing w/ customers on "can you share logs" and it takes forever. Can we standardize what we ask for when SDK calls fail (timeouts/429/stream disconnect)? Specifically request_id + SDK version. What’s the best set of questions for oncall/support?

Dev Patel: +1. We need a crisp macro. Right now folks ask for "request id" but customers don’t know where to find it, esp in streaming.

Ibrahim Khan: For parity work we’re making `request_id` show up in typed errors, but until everyone upgrades we should ask for BOTH:
- request_id (from error object if present)
- OR the response header `x-request-id` if they can capture it
Also ask for SDK language + exact version + config (retries/timeouts).

Allison Grant: Agree. For oncall triage, minimum viable dataset:
1) request_id (or multiple, if it’s retrying)
2) timestamp (with timezone) + region (if they know)
3) endpoint + model + streaming vs non-streaming
4) SDK name/version + runtime (Python/Node/Go version)
5) retry config: max_attempts, backoff, whether they respect Retry-After
6) timeout config (overall/connect/read if applicable)
If it’s streaming: do they see partial tokens? where does it stop? any proxy?

Dev Patel: Can we give them copy/paste instructions to get SDK version quickly?

Ibrahim Khan: Yup. Something like:
- Python: `python -c "import redwood; print(redwood.__version__)"`
- Node: `npm ls @redwood-ai/sdk` (or `pnpm why ...`)
- Go: `go list -m all | grep redwood` (or whatever module path is)
We should keep it generic in the macro and link to docs.

Monica Patel: Let’s turn this into a playbook update. We already have INT-1182 open for request_id visibility; we can also update the oncall checklist + support macro now.
Action: I’ll ask Dev + Owen to propose wording, we’ll review in DevEx.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: please also ask which SDK major/minor they’re on (pre- vs post-standardization). We’re going to have a wave of mixed behavior for a bit.
Maybe add: “Are you on Python >= X / TS >= Y / Go >= Z?” (we can fill versions once the stable tags are out).

Nadia Rahman: Also worth asking for retry_count if they have logs. We’re adding standardized fields soon but today customers can log attempts themselves.
Suggested question: “Do you see multiple attempts for the same user action? If yes, approx how many?”

Marcus Lin: Security reminder for the macro: explicitly say “Do NOT send API keys/bearer tokens.”
If they paste headers, ask them to redact `Authorization`.
If we recommend debug logging, ensure it’s clear Redwood SDKs redact secrets but customer app logs might not.

Dev Patel: Draft macro v0 (feedback welcome):
"To help us investigate, please reply with:
1) Your SDK language + version (and your runtime version, e.g. Node 20 / Python 3.11 / Go 1.22)
2) request_id for a failed call (from the error message/object, or from the `x-request-id` response header). If it retried, include *all* request_ids you have.
3) Approx timestamp + timezone
4) Endpoint + model + whether streaming was enabled
5) Your retry + timeout settings (max attempts, any custom backoff, timeout values)
6) If streaming: did you receive partial output? any corporate proxy/VPN?
Please do not include API keys or Authorization headers."

Allison Grant: Looks good. One more thing: ask if they’re using our OpenAI-compat endpoint vs native, since error shapes differed historically.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Add a line: “Which endpoint style are you using: Redwood native SDK calls vs OpenAI-compatible base_url?”

Owen Phillips: This is great. I’ll update the oncall runbook doc + the Zendesk macro and link it in #support.
Also going to add a section "How to find request_id" with examples per SDK once the new errors land.

Monica Patel: Please file a small ticket under the parity epic for the macro + runbook update so it doesn’t get lost. Tag me + Dev.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: When you add versions, ping #eng-releases so we keep them aligned with the actual ship vehicles.

Dev Patel: Will do. I’ll open INT-1199 follow-up w/ the macro text + request_id instructions and assign to Owen/me.

Owen Phillips: Thanks all. This should cut the back-and-forth a lot during incidents.

Allison Grant: Looks good. One more thing: ask if they’re using our OpenAI-compat endpoint vs native, since error shapes differed historically.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Add a line: “Which endpoint style are you using: Redwood native SDK calls vs OpenAI-compatible base_url?”

Owen Phillips: This is great. I’ll update the oncall runbook doc + the Zendesk macro and link it in #support.
Also going to add a section "How to find request_id" with examples per SDK once the new errors land.

Monica Patel: Please file a small ticket under the parity epic for the macro + runbook update so it doesn’t get lost. Tag me + Dev.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: When you add versions, ping #eng-releases so we keep them aligned with the actual ship vehicles.

Dev Patel: Will do. I’ll open INT-1199 follow-up w/ the macro text + request_id instructions and assign to Owen/me.

Owen Phillips: Thanks all. This should cut the back-and-forth a lot during incidents.

title: #eng-infra: 1760710033
preview:
Sahana Iyer: heads up: the signature verification integration tests are now the slowest step in `private-installer` CI 😬

Sahana Iyer: `bundle-verify-integration` job went from ~6m to ~28-35m after we added tamper/replay/downgrade cases. We need to get this back under ~10m or we’re gonna start timing out in release train.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118244 for redwood-private-installer / main failed
ci-bot: Step `bundle-verify-integration` exceeded soft timeout (30m). Log: https://buildkite.example.redwood/...

Devon Price: looking at the logs now. a lot of time is `sha256sum` over big fixtures + re-generating offline bundle each test case.

Ethan Park: yeah, each test currently:
1) builds bundle (tar + generate manifest)
2) signs manifest
3) unpacks
4) modifies artifact
5) runs preflight

Ethan Park: so we’re doing the expensive steps N times.

Jordan Lee: manifest canonicalization step is also re-running every time. not huge but it adds up.

Sahana Iyer: can we make a “golden” bundle fixture once per job and then derive tampered variants cheaply?

Devon Price: +1. also the fixture includes a big image tarball (we added it to simulate offline image import). hashing that is killing us.

Connor O'Brien: release side: we don’t actually need a 3GB image tar to validate signature semantics. we need to validate that we *would* hash the referenced artifact, but the artifact can be small in tests.

Marcus Lin: from security POV, as long as the test still exercises:
- content hash validation
- signature verification
- fail-closed behavior
- downgrade protection
we can shrink the bytes.

Sean Gallagher: also worth splitting “fast unit-ish verification tests” vs “true end-to-end on a full bundle”. run the full bundle one nightly?

Ethan Park: we already have unit tests for canonicalization + envelope parsing. the integration suite is meant to be “bundle on disk + preflight CLI”.

Ethan Park: but we could do:
- one e2e uses a “medium” bundle (still realistic layout)
- tamper variants derived from that bundle via file copy + single edit

Sahana Iyer: what’s the slowest operation by flame graph / rough breakdown?

Devon Price: rough from build logs:
- `create_bundle()` ~9-12m (mostly tar + sha256s)
- `verify_bundle()` ~30-60s each
- repeated 12 times = pain

Devon Price: also `docker save` in one fixture is ~4m. why are we doing docker save here?

Ethan Park: legacy from older offline install tests. for signing, we only need to test that the manifest lists artifacts + hashes and we verify them offline.

Jordan Lee: can we replace docker save fixture with just a dummy file named `images/redwood-runtime.tar` that is small?

Marcus Lin: yes, but keep the path + semantics. the test should ensure that *changing that file* triggers hash mismatch.

Sahana Iyer: ok. proposal:
1) replace heavy image fixture with small deterministic tar (like `tar --mtime=0 --sort=name ...`)
2) generate base bundle once, cache it across test cases
3) for each test: copy base bundle dir (hardlink?) and mutate only one file

Sahana Iyer: for (2) we can do `TestMain` / shared setup if in Go.

Ethan Park: suite is in Go. we can use `sync.Once` and write base bundle into `$TMPDIR/base_bundle`.

Devon Price: copying the bundle dir might still be expensive. can we do copy-on-write? linux hardlinks via `cp -al`?

Sahana Iyer: Buildkite agents are linux, so `cp -al` should work. but careful: mutating file should break hardlink (need to copy the file you mutate, not write in-place).

Jordan Lee: for tamper cases we usually modify manifest or one artifact. we can do:
- copy tree as hardlinks
- for the file to modify, `cp` it first to break link, then edit

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Connor O'Brien: also add `BUILDKITE_CACHE`/artifact caching? we could upload the base bundle as build artifact for the step and have other steps consume it.

Sahana Iyer: within a single step we can probably avoid cross-step caching. cross-build caching is tricky (branch isolation) but possible.

Devon Price: simplest win is: stop regenerating bundle per test. right now it’s literally in each test function.

Ethan Park: i can refactor to build once.

Sean Gallagher: do we have `-run TestBundleVerifyTamper.*` set to run serial? go tests default parallel at package level but not within suite.

Devon Price: we turned off `t.Parallel()` previously because tests were stomping on temp dirs.

Sahana Iyer: if we make temp dirs unique per test (and base bundle read-only), we can turn `t.Parallel()` back on for mutation/verify steps.

Ethan Park: verify step reads from disk + computes hashes; parallel might be ok. but CPU-bound hashing can blow up. if we shrink fixture, it’s fine.

Jordan Lee: also we can short-circuit hashing in tests by using smaller artifacts, as discussed.

Marcus Lin: just don’t accidentally create tests that pass even if verification skips hashing. we should keep a test that ensures hash mismatch is caught.

Ethan Park: yep.

Sahana Iyer: what about caching computed hashes? manifest already contains expected hashes. verification computes actual hashes. but for test runtime, we can keep artifact sizes small.

Devon Price: also: our “base bundle” generator currently re-hashes *every file* even when we only need a few artifacts for the integration tests.

Devon Price: could create a “test bundle builder” that emits minimal layout:
- manifest.json
- manifest.sig
- charts/ (tiny)
- binaries/ (tiny)
- sbom/ pointer file

Connor O'Brien: +1. our CI should validate semantics, not the full production payload.

Sahana Iyer: ok. action items:
- Ethan: refactor suite to build base bundle once per package; use hardlink copy per test + break link on mutated file
- Devon: swap heavy fixtures (docker save) -> deterministic tiny tar/file
- Sahana: adjust Buildkite step timeouts + add timing logs (per phase)

Ethan Park: i’ll take the refactor. estimate: today + tomorrow.

Devon Price: i’ll patch fixtures now. probably behind a flag so we don’t lose the “realistic” path names.

Marcus Lin: please add a comment in code about why fixtures are small (perf) + what security properties are still validated.

Sean Gallagher: once it’s down, can we set an SLO for this job? like <12m p95 on main.

Sahana Iyer: yep. will add to infra dashboard after we stabilize.

Devon Price: quick note: deterministic tar creation should use:
`tar --sort=name --mtime='UTC 2020-01-01' --owner=0 --group=0 --numeric-owner -cf ...`
so hashes are stable.

Jordan Lee: love it. also ensure json canonicalization for manifest is stable. our tests currently write json with stdlib which doesn’t guarantee key order if map-based.

Ethan Park: base bundle generator uses our canonical writer (sorted keys). good.

Connor O'Brien: ping me if we need to change the release gate thresholds. but ideally this doesn’t block RC builds anymore.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 started for redwood-private-installer / branch `ethan/shared-bundle-fixture`

Sahana Iyer: thanks all. let’s regroup after we have numbers.

Ethan Park: update: refactor done locally. base bundle built once. tamper tests run in parallel with isolated dirs. runtime on my laptop: 4m -> 55s.

Devon Price: fixture patch landed too. docker save removed. the “image tar” is now 8KB deterministic file. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: reviewed the changes. looks fine. keep one test that ensures changing `images/redwood-runtime.tar` triggers `HASH_MISMATCH` and we’re good.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. once CI confirms, i’ll tighten timeout back down and add an alert if it regresses.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 passed
ci-bot: Step timings:
- bundle-verify-integration: 6m12s (was 33m41s)

Sahana Iyer: ✅ perf is back. i’ll file a follow-up to add a nightly “full-size bundle smoke” so we still catch weird tar/unpack issues without slowing main.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. this unblocks the release train gate. thanks.

title: #eng-releases: 1760674555
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off key rotation drill dry-run for offline bundle signing. Goal is to walk the runbook end-to-end (generate new key, update trust store, dual-sign, cut RC, verify installer preflight, capture evidence). Drop notes here as we go.

Sahana Iyer: Here’s the sequence I’m following (from draft runbook):
1) create new signing key in HSM slot + export pubkey
2) update installer trust store (ship in next installer build)
3) update CI signing job to dual-sign manifest (`manifest.sig` old + `manifest.sig.new` or combined envelope)
4) verify `rpi bundle inspect --verify` passes with both keys
5) cut a Private RC bundle and test downgrade prevention still works

Devon Price: FYI I pulled the current signing job config from `private-release-signing.yml`. It assumes a single `SIGNING_KEY_ALIAS=offline-bundle-prod` and writes `manifest.sig` only. Dual-sign isn’t wired.

Hanae Suzuki: On key creation: HSM emulator in staging is flaky again. I can generate the new key, but the export step is failing with permission denied (looks like the service account token isn’t scoped for the new alias).

ReleaseBot: CI job `private/offline-bundle/sign` failed on staging.
Step: `redwood-sign export-public-key --alias offline-bundle-2025q4-rot-01`
Error: `403: not authorized for alias offline-bundle-2025q4-rot-01`

Sahana Iyer: That’s already a “what went wrong”: the pipeline role bindings are hardcoded to the old alias. We need an automation path to create alias + bind perms + audit it.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also from a threat model POV, we *want* explicit approval gates on creating a new signing key alias. But it needs to be repeatable and auditable (no ad-hoc console clicking).

Connor O'Brien: We’ll treat this as: key creation is manual w/ approvals, but the permission binding + evidence capture should be scripted.

Hanae Suzuki: I can unblock by adding the alias to the allowed list for the signing operator role in staging, but that’s manual.

Connor O'Brien: Do it for the drill (staging only), and let’s note the gap.

Hanae Suzuki: Done. Alias `offline-bundle-2025q4-rot-01` created, pubkey exported. Where do we want to store the pubkey artifact for the drill? (internal repo vs release bucket)

Devon Price: For now we’ve been embedding keys in the installer repo under `trust/keys/` and shipping compiled trust store. For evidence, we also attach the pubkey + key-id to the release ticket.

Ethan Park: Installer trust store update is the next blocker: current released installer only trusts `offline-bundle-prod-2024` key id. So any bundle signed solely by the new key fails preflight with `UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY`.

Ethan Park: I tried locally by swapping the trust store file:
```
$ rpi preflight --bundle ./bundle-rc.tgz --airgapped
ERROR BNDL-VERIFY-002: signing key not trusted
  key_id: offline-bundle-2025q4-rot-01
  trust_store: /opt/redwood/etc/trust-store.json
  remediation: update installer trust store or use break-glass (policy-gated)
```
So we can’t validate the full “rotate key then cut bundle” flow without cutting an installer build first.

Connor O'Brien: Yep, sequencing issue. For the drill we should do the correct real-world order:
A) ship installer update that includes both keys (old+new)
B) start dual-signing bundles
C) after N releases, drop old key

Sahana Iyer: Agree. The runbook draft currently has key generation first, which is fine, but it should explicitly say “don’t publish bundles signed w/ new key only until installer trust store is in the field.”

Olga Petrov: From compliance side, we need a clean story for customers: there is a transition window where installer trusts both keys. Also we should document how air-gapped customers get the updated trust store (new installer media vs separate trust store update package).

Daniel Carter: For evidence mapping: we should capture (1) approvals for rotation, (2) hash of trust store content shipped, (3) signing logs for first dual-signed release, (4) verification test output. Right now we don’t have a “bundle verification test output artifact” saved anywhere.

Devon Price: On dual-sign: we have two options:
- produce `manifest.sig` and `manifest.sig.next` (two detached sig files)
- produce a single envelope that contains signatures array
Installer currently expects single detached sig file.

Ethan Park: Installer has PRs for “trust store + key rollover” and “detached signature layout” but we didn’t implement multi-sig envelope. Supporting two sig files is easiest. But manifest schema needs to declare which signature file(s) to verify.

Marcus Lin: Multi-sig is safer long-term (explicit threshold / multiple signers), but for rotation we can do “accept any of the trusted keys” with a single sig file. That implies we keep signing with old key until the trust store update propagates.

Connor O'Brien: That seems like the right path for now: ship trust store update first, then move signing key for `manifest.sig` to new key on a later release (not dual-sign). BUT we still want the ability to verify older bundles after rotation, so old key stays trusted for a deprecation period.

Sahana Iyer: Noted. That changes the drill outcome: we can validate (a) trust store can contain multiple keys, (b) installer chooses correct key based on signature key-id, (c) downgrade prevention still works when key changes.

Sean Gallagher: Operational note: any time we flip the signing key used for new bundles, Support will see a spike in `unknown key` errors from customers who didn’t update installer. We need an alert + a canned response + a “check installer version vs bundle version” checklist.

Logan Wright: Also audit event currently logs `verify_success=true/false` but doesn’t include `key_id`. That’s going to be painful during rotation.

Ethan Park: We can add `key_id` and `trust_store_version` to the local audit event. There’s already a field placeholder in the schema. (This came up in PR-24947 too.)

Neha Kapoor: For SRE: we should monitor signing pipeline failures and time-to-issue signed bundle. Rotation days are high risk. Add a “rotation mode” dashboard annotation when we start.

Connor O'Brien: OK, continuing drill with updated sequencing. Action: generate installer build candidate w/ dual trust store.

Devon Price: I made a local branch to add the new pubkey to `trust-store.json` and bumped `trust_store_version: 2025.10-rot1`.

Sahana Iyer: Heads up: when I built installer, preflight still failed with `not-yet-valid` on a test signature where we used the x509-backed key wrapper. The cert NotBefore was set to 10 minutes in the future (clock skew on the build runner?).

ReleaseBot: Test `verify_signature_time_window` failed.
Error: `x509: certificate is not yet valid`
Runner clock: `2025-10-17T03:21:05Z`
Cert NotBefore: `2025-10-17T03:31:12Z`

Hanae Suzuki: That’s from the cert generation defaulting to “NotBefore=now” but the HSM service clock is slightly ahead of the runner clock. For air-gapped customers, this exact scenario happens a lot.

Marcus Lin: We should avoid relying on tight validity windows for the *signing cert* in offline verification. If we keep x509, set NotBefore to something conservative (e.g., now - 24h). Or switch to raw key signatures without x509 validity checks.

Connor O'Brien: For this project we already have that time-skew bugfix PR (24961). The drill confirms it’s real in build infra too, not just customer envs.

Ethan Park: In installer we added “time override for diagnostics only” flag, but default behavior is fail closed. For rotation, we need the release pipeline to not generate certs that fail validation in a normal system.

Sahana Iyer: I can fix in the signing job: when generating the signing cert wrapper, set `not_before = now - 1d`. But that should be in the signing tool itself to avoid drift.

Devon Price: Another thing: the `trust-store.json` is being serialized with different whitespace/order depending on the builder. If we ever sign the trust store itself (we probably should), we need canonicalization.

Connor O'Brien: Adding to follow-ups.

Olga Petrov: For customer comms: we need to explicitly address “time source” and “clock skew” in the rotation docs. Security teams will ask why signatures can be “not yet valid” in an air gap.

Daniel Carter: Evidence note: for the drill, capture screenshots/logs of (1) new key created, (2) approvals, (3) trust store diff, (4) a successful verification run on an offline machine. Right now we have logs sprinkled across Slack/CI.

Sean Gallagher: Also, runbook needs an explicit rollback step: if new key causes widespread failures, revert signing to old key, keep both trusted, and ship a corrective installer update.

Connor O'Brien: Summarizing “what went wrong” so far:
- CI/HSM permissions tied to a fixed alias -> rotation breaks until manual RBAC update
- Runbook sequencing unclear: can’t publish bundles signed by new key until trust store is distributed
- Time skew caused x509 “not yet valid” failures in CI (not just customer env)
- Missing audit fields (`key_id`, trust_store_version) makes rotation triage hard
- Trust store canonicalization / reproducibility concerns (future)

Connor O'Brien: Proposed follow-ups (owners pls confirm):
1) Scripted rotation scaffold: create alias + bind minimal perms + emit audit evidence bundle (Hanae + Sahana)
2) Update runbook sequencing + add rollback section (Connor + Sean)
3) Signing tool: set conservative NotBefore or move away from x509 validity check for bundle signature verification (Hanae + Marcus)
4) Installer audit event additions: include `key_id`, `trust_store_version`, and “break-glass used” marker (Logan + Ethan)
5) Release checklist: require installer version gate before switching active signing key (Connor + Devon)

Hanae Suzuki: Confirm for (1) and (3). For (1) we should also require approval artifact (who approved rotation). I’ll draft a “rotation request” template.

Sahana Iyer: Confirm for (1). I’ll add a CI job that validates the signing operator role has access to the *next* alias before we attempt rotation.

Marcus Lin: Confirm for (3). I’d prefer we don’t treat x509 validity as a hard gate for the signing identity in offline mode; the signature is the integrity mechanism, time validity is optional/fragile offline. We can keep certs for identity/chain, but don’t fail installs because of NotBefore drift.

Ethan Park: Confirm for (4). We can add fields without breaking schema; exporter mapping might need an update too.

Logan Wright: Confirm. I’ll coordinate w/ audit-log-exporter folks so `bundle_verification` includes those fields.

Sean Gallagher: Confirm for (2). I’ll also add a Support note section: how to diagnose `UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY` and what to ask customers for (installer version, trust store hash, `rpi bundle inspect` output).

Neha Kapoor: Please add an SRE note: on rotation day, we need an on-call aware handoff + explicit “rotation start/stop” messages in `#eng-releases`.

Devon Price: Confirm for (5). I’ll update the release train checklist so we can’t flip the signing alias unless the installer RC containing both keys is already published.

Olga Petrov: For (2)/(5): loop me in on wording for the customer-facing policy: how long old keys remain trusted, and what constitutes “revocation.”

Daniel Carter: I’ll put together an “evidence packet” outline for the drill so next time we aren’t hunting logs.

Connor O'Brien: Cool. I’ll open/attach tasks to the rotation ticket set (ENG-4825 / ENG-4862) and link back here. Next drill after we land audit fields + fix the NotBefore skew.

ReleaseBot: Reminder set by Connor O'Brien: “Key rotation drill v2” in 14 days.
