Like `hubspot_payload_policy_violations_total` (should be 0 always). If >0, page the oncall + auto-disable routing.

Talia Benson: Capturing this for the runbook. Can we agree on *alert thresholds* (page vs ticket)? Proposed:
P0 page:
- HubSpot 429s sustained (>= 10% of requests for 10m) OR retry queue depth continuously increasing for 15m
- End-to-end lag p95 > 6h (excluding backfills)
- Task create failures > 2% for 10m
P1 ticket:
- 5xx spikes but recovering
- row writes drop >50% vs 7d median
Thoughts?

Logan Wright: 6h lag feels high given we’re doing daily windows, but the expectation from sales is “I should see tasks same day.” I’d set:
- ticket at p95 > 2h for 2 consecutive hours
- page at p95 > 6h for 2 consecutive hours
and explicitly suppress during backfill.

Mei Lin: +1 to suppression during backfills. We can tag runs with `window_type=daily|backfill` and key alerts off daily only.
Also add:
- `aggregation_window_complete_timestamp` gauge (epoch) so we can compute freshness in Grafana easily.

Ethan Park: On 429s: HubSpot rate limits are bursty. I’d rather page on “routing stopped” symptoms:
- `hubspot_task_create_success_rate` < 95% for 15m (page)
- `hubspot_retry_queue_depth` > 5k and increasing (page)
And a warning when 429 ratio > 5% for 10m (ticket) so we tune batching.

Caleb Johnson: For dropped events: page if `telemetry_events_dropped_total{reason!=schema_invalid}` increases by > 1k in 10m AND lag p95 > 1h. Otherwise we’ll page every time a client SDK rolls.

Priya Natarajan: This is great. Let’s lock a minimal set for day-1 oncall:
1) HubSpot delivery health (success rate, retries, 429/5xx)
2) E2E lag
3) DLQ depth / dropped events
4) Job/run success
Anything beyond that can be phase 2.

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a “volume sanity” panel:
- `signals_created_total` by type
- `signals_routed_total` by region
If we suddenly double our routing volume, that’s usually a threshold regression.

Talia Benson: Owners?
- HubSpot metrics/alerts: @Ethan Park
- Ingestion lag/DLQ/drops: @Caleb Johnson
- Aggregation freshness + writes: @Mei Lin
- Overall dashboards + PD integration: I can coordinate but need a DRI for the service

Priya Natarajan: DRI for service ops is Logan (Telemetry & Analytics) for this pilot. Ethan owns HubSpot path. I’ll be escalation for platform.

DeployBot: reminder: PR-28630 (metrics hooks) merged to main. Staging deploy scheduled 18:00 PT.

Logan Wright: Dashboard placeholders (will fill once Grafana panels are live):
- Signal Router Overview: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/overview (TBD)
- HubSpot Delivery: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/hubspot (TBD)
- Ingestion + DLQ: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/ingestion (TBD)
Runbook placeholder: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/PLG+Signal+Router+Oncall+Runbook (TBD)

Marcus Lin: +1 on runbook including “kill switch” steps if payload policy violations fire.

Priya Natarajan: Action items:
- Ethan: define alert rules for HubSpot success rate + retry queue depth (and add to PD service)
- Mei/Caleb: freshness + DLQ alerts with backfill suppression
- Talia: create runbook skeleton + link to INT-1843 + suppression guidance
Let’s aim to have alerts in place before we expand pilot cohort next week.

title: #eng-infra: 1747211044
preview:
Bruno Silva: Heads up: Terraform module changes for KMS are ready for review. This is the follow-on to the least-privilege work + rotation role split. Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-terraform/pull/611 ("add least-privilege KMS IAM modules").

Bruno Silva: TL;DR
- New submodules: `kms_runtime_role` (Decrypt/DataKey) and `kms_rotation_role` (DescribeKey + CreateGrant/PutKeyPolicy bits depending on cloud).
- Default behavior stays close to existing, but we *will* add new outputs and a couple of variables.
- Need to decide version bump + compatibility notes for existing Private customers.

Jordan Lee: looking now. Do we currently have customers pinning the module version in their Private deploy repos? If yes, we need to be careful about a breaking major.

Bruno Silva: yep, most Private customers pin `redwood/private` terraform modules. Some are pinned by installer-generated `versions.tf` too. That’s why I’m trying to avoid a surprise break.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: the big concern is rollout sequence. If we ship module changes that tighten IAM and someone applies before the runtime wrapper cutover is fully deployed, we get decrypt failures.

Bruno Silva: +1. In PR I kept the existing policy attachments as defaults, and added a new `enable_separate_rotation_principal` flag (default false) so we don’t force split principals immediately.

Jordan Lee: I see it. Variable name: `separate_rotation_principal_enabled` in one file and `enable_separate_rotation_principal` in README. Can we standardize? :)

Bruno Silva: good catch, will fix.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we should still message this as “recommended split roles” and call out that rotation actions should not run under the runtime decrypt principal. But I’m ok with defaulting to current behavior for compatibility.

Allison Grant: also please make sure module exports the role ARN consistently. Observability hooks expect the label `kms_principal` to match what we document (role ARN string).

Bruno Silva: module outputs right now:
- existing: `kms_role_arn`
- new: `kms_runtime_role_arn`, `kms_rotation_role_arn`
I left `kms_role_arn` as an alias to runtime for now.

Jordan Lee: that alias helps. Please document that `kms_role_arn` is deprecated but still present.

Vivek Kulkarni: for Private: installer will generate terraform config. We can update installer templates to use the new outputs, but we need a path for existing customers who don’t upgrade installer immediately.

Bruno Silva: exactly. Proposed compatibility plan:
1) Terraform module release: minor bump (e.g. 3.6.0 -> 3.7.0) w/ new roles behind flag, keep old outputs.
2) Installer update references new outputs but continues to accept old variables if present.
3) After 1-2 quarters, we can do a major bump that removes deprecated outputs.

Connor O'Brien: release process question: do we publish these modules with semver tags? if so, we should treat output removals as major.

Bruno Silva: yes, internal semver tags. That’s why I’m leaning minor now.

Sean Gallagher: can we also add a “safety check” note: apply in staging first; verify decrypt calls before tightening? People will copy/paste.

Bruno Silva: yep. Adding README section: “Upgrade notes (Private existing customers)” with a checklist:
- Ensure KMS wrapper + circuit breaker changes deployed (>= runtime build X)
- Apply terraform
- Confirm dashboards: KMS AccessDenied=0, latency stable
- Then enable `enable_separate_rotation_principal=true` in a follow-up change

Jordan Lee: please be explicit about permissions.
Runtime principal should need:
- `kms:Decrypt`
- `kms:GenerateDataKey` (or `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` depending)
Rotation principal should need:
- `kms:DescribeKey`
- and whatever is used to update alias/key versioning from our control plane (we generally don’t want `kms:PutKeyPolicy` unless customer model requires it)

Marcus Lin: +1. Also mention: customers may have SCPs / org policies; we should avoid adding optional permissions by default.

Bruno Silva: PR currently uses a conservative base policy and exposes `extra_kms_actions_runtime` / `extra_kms_actions_rotation` lists if needed.

Allison Grant: any renames to metric tags needed? If the role changes, we should ensure dashboards don’t break. But that’s probably on the app side.

Sean Gallagher: oncall pain point: customers apply Terraform and then file a ticket with an AccessDenied. If we can add a note: “common failure mode: key policy doesn’t allow new role ARN” that’d save cycles.

Bruno Silva: adding to README troubleshooting:
- “AccessDenied after applying: update key policy to trust the runtime role ARN; if enabling split roles, ensure both ARNs are trusted during transition.”

Vivek Kulkarni: can we align this with installer preflight validation? The installer PR adds checks (I think PR-392). If terraform changes cause new role ARN, preflight should warn if key policy doesn’t include it.

Bruno Silva: yep, I’ll sync w/ that. https://github.com/redwood-private-installer/pull/392 is the one.

Jordan Lee: one more: module currently changes the default session name / role path? Some customers rely on predictable IAM paths.

Bruno Silva: no role path change. Names will be stable unless `name_prefix` differs. I did add suffixes `-runtime` and `-rotation` when split enabled.

Connor O'Brien: what’s the plan for existing customers who already have a single role and don’t want terraform to replace it? Will enabling split force replacement?

Bruno Silva: enabling split creates *additional* role resources, doesn’t destroy existing. If they keep split disabled, resources are unchanged.

Sean Gallagher: good. Also please avoid “force new” on the role by changing assume role policy unexpectedly.

Bruno Silva: no assume role policy changes when split disabled.

Marcus Lin: version bump proposal sounds right: minor bump now with deprecation warnings.

Jordan Lee: can you add a changelog entry that explicitly says “no breaking changes if you don’t enable split”? People skim.

Bruno Silva: will do.

Allison Grant: if we can get a single canonical snippet for Private upgrade docs, I can link it from the KMS dashboards page too.

Bruno Silva: I’ll paste draft snippet here once README updates are in.

Sean Gallagher: request: include a note about staged rollout: enable split roles only after verifying dual-read / rotation window logic if they’re doing key rotation same week.

Bruno Silva: added note: “Don’t combine IAM tightening + key rotation cutover in the same maintenance window unless you have rollback.”

Vivek Kulkarni: :thumbsup: also note some customers do quarterly rotations w/ audit evidence; they’ll ask if this changes their rotation procedure.

Marcus Lin: we can answer: “no change required; split roles is recommended hardening; rotation procedure is the same, just uses different principal.”

Bruno Silva: ok, action items from this thread:
- Fix variable naming consistency
- Add README: upgrade notes, troubleshooting, staged rollout checklist
- Add changelog entry for minor bump
- Confirm no unexpected role replacement
I’ll push updates and re-request review on PR-611 today.

Jordan Lee: thanks. I’ll do detailed policy review once updated.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll skim for rollout hazards / defaults.

Connor O'Brien: once merged, ping me and I’ll coordinate tagging + internal announcement so Private folks don’t get surprised.

Bruno Silva: will do.

title: #eng-platform: 1747160501
preview:
Ethan Park: starting a thread on HubSpot auth for the signal router service — OAuth scopes, token rotation, secret storage, audit trail requirements. we need to lock this before we merge the integration PRs.

David Kim: :wave: from HubSpot side: we should use a HubSpot public app w/ OAuth (not a private app token). private app tokens are easy but auditing + rotation is worse and security is going to hate it.

Marcus Lin: +1. private app tokens are basically long-lived bearer secrets. OAuth w/ refresh tokens is the right default. also want least-privilege scopes.

Ethan Park: ok. current plan in PR uses OAuth refresh token stored in Secrets Manager, access token cached in memory w/ TTL. question is: which scopes are actually required for MVP? we’re doing:
- company lookup by domain / name
- upsert company properties (custom properties)
- create timeline note/activity
- create task assigned to owner

Tyler Benson: from marketing ops/workflows: we’ll also have workflows that read the properties + list membership, but the service itself shouldn’t need workflow scopes.

Isabelle Laurent: sales ops angle: we need ownership assignment to respect existing owner + round robin fallback. does that require any extra scopes to set owner id? and do we need to read owners?

David Kim: updating owner on a company uses `crm.objects.companies.write` and setting `hubspot_owner_id` property. reading owners list is separate (`crm.objects.owners.read`). if you want to map email -> ownerId deterministically you’ll need that. alternative: keep mapping in our side (config) and avoid the owners API.

Logan Wright: would prefer config mapping for pilot (NA only). keep CRM calls minimal to avoid 429s. owner mapping can be a static table keyed by region/team.

Ethan Park: makes sense. but we still need to preserve existing owner. that means we need to read company properties including owner id.

David Kim: yup, `crm.objects.companies.read`.

Marcus Lin: for tasks + notes: tasks are engagement objects. scope is `crm.objects.tasks.write` + `crm.objects.tasks.read` if we ever query them. notes are `crm.objects.notes.write`. there’s also `crm.objects.contacts.*` if we tie to contacts, but MVP is company-level right?

Ethan Park: MVP is company-level. we attach note + task to company record.

David Kim: attaching associations might require `crm.schemas.*`? no, just using standard associations should be fine. but you may need `crm.objects.*.write` for the object types you create.

Caleb Johnson: do we need any scopes for “timeline events”? hubspot has that “timeline” API but I think we’re just using notes.

David Kim: correct. don’t use Timeline Extensions for MVP. notes are enough and show up in the record timeline.

Priya Natarajan: can we write down a minimal scope set and get security signoff? I want this stable so we don’t have to redo the app later.

Ethan Park: draft minimal scopes:
```
crm.objects.companies.read
crm.objects.companies.write
crm.objects.notes.write
crm.objects.tasks.write
oauth
```
optional:
```
crm.objects.owners.read
```

Marcus Lin: question: does HubSpot require the `oauth` scope explicitly? usually it’s implied but leaving it in docs is fine.

David Kim: in HubSpot app config, you select “scopes” and OAuth is implied. but in our internal docs we should list “OAuth app w/ refresh token flow” as requirement.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: looping in. couple requirements from security:
1) refresh token must be stored in a managed secrets store (AWS Secrets Manager ok; Vault ok). encrypted at rest + restricted IAM.
2) no tokens in logs. redact + structured logging.
3) token rotation policy: refresh token rotation at least every 90 days OR on employee/offboarding / suspected compromise. confirm hubspot refresh tokens can be revoked + reissued w/o downtime.
4) audit trail: record every token exchange + every API call outcome? at minimum: who/what created/updated CRM objects (service identity), correlation IDs, and an immutable log trail (CloudWatch + retention).

Ethan Park: on (3) — we can revoke the app install and re-install to re-issue refresh token. but that’s disruptive. is there a way to rotate refresh token without uninstall?

David Kim: HubSpot issues new refresh token on re-auth. you can run the OAuth flow again and swap the token. not as disruptive as uninstall; but still “manual” unless we build a token rotation job.

Marcus Lin: so implement “rotate by re-auth” playbook. store token versioned, allow dual token overlap during swap? easiest: write new secret value, deploy picks it up, old one invalidated later.

Priya Natarajan: we should design the secret fetch to support hot reload (polling) or at least quick restart.

Ethan Park: PR currently loads secret at startup. we can add periodic refresh (e.g., every 10m) to pick up secret changes + avoid redeploy.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: periodic refresh is good, but be careful: if HubSpot rate limits token exchange, don’t re-auth too frequently. only refresh access tokens as needed, not refresh tokens.

Caleb Johnson: also note: we saw HubSpot 429s in other contexts. token refresh should be resilient — exponential backoff + jitter, and queue writes.

Logan Wright: +1. plus add metrics: `hubspot_token_refresh_success`, `hubspot_token_refresh_fail`, and `hubspot_auth_401` count. auth 401 spikes are the canary for token rotation issues.

Tyler Benson: re: audit trail — ops needs to be able to answer “why did this account get outreach.” can we include signal id in note body + task subject so it’s traceable?

Ethan Park: yes. note template includes `plg_signal_id`, `signal_type`, `window_start/end`, and `router_version`.

Isabelle Laurent: and please include “source = product signal router” so reps don’t think it’s marketing automation. also if the service updates owner, log it.

Marcus Lin: secrets storage: prefer AWS Secrets Manager with KMS CMK, restricted to service role, no human read. if humans need rotation, use a break-glass role with approval.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: agree. also store HubSpot app client secret in same secret bundle. don’t keep it in CI vars.

David Kim: HubSpot app creds: client_id, client_secret. refresh token is per portal install. we’ll have one portal (Redwood’s) for now. if we add sandbox portal later, we’ll need separate install + separate refresh token.

Ethan Park: ok, so secret structure (conceptually):
- HUBSPOT_CLIENT_ID
- HUBSPOT_CLIENT_SECRET
- HUBSPOT_REFRESH_TOKEN
- HUBSPOT_PORTAL_ID (optional)

Marcus Lin: don’t store as env vars in plaintext. load them at runtime.

Priya Natarajan: what about “who did the API call” visibility in HubSpot? will it show as “App: Redwood Signal Router”?

David Kim: yes, changes will be attributed to the app (good). tasks/notes will show created by integration.

SecurityBot: Reminder: Security review checklist for third-party SaaS integrations: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/security/saas-integrations-checklist (Token storage, logging, rotation, vendor scopes, incident response)

Ethan Park: action items:
1) Ethan: update ENG PR to (a) minimize scopes to list above, (b) add secret hot-reload (poll), (c) add auth-related metrics.
2) David: confirm exact scopes required for notes+tasks association to company (any hidden gotchas) + share HubSpot app config screenshot.
3) Marcus/Aisha: sign off on 90-day refresh token rotation runbook + break-glass access.
4) Tyler/Isabelle: confirm we don’t need contact scopes for MVP + owner mapping approach.

Tyler Benson: marketing ops ok with company-only for MVP. we can add contacts later for lifecycle emails.

Isabelle Laurent: sales ops ok with config mapping for NA pilot. we’ll revisit owner API if we expand teams/regions.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: once you push the changes, tag me + Marcus. include a short “data in/out” section in the ADR: what’s stored, what’s logged, retention.

Marcus Lin: also add a log scrubber test (unit test) to ensure no token strings appear. we’ve been burned by this before.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: will do. will post PR link in this thread when ready.

David Kim: fyi HubSpot OAuth scopes naming sometimes differs in UI vs API docs. I’ll send the canonical list.

Logan Wright: last ask: make sure we set a reasonable access token refresh buffer (refresh when <60s remaining) to avoid concurrent refresh stampede under load.

Ethan Park: yep. will use a singleflight lock around refresh.

Priya Natarajan: thanks all — keep this tight, we want minimal risk before pilot rollout.

Marcus Lin: question: does HubSpot require the `oauth` scope explicitly? usually it’s implied but leaving it in docs is fine.

David Kim: in HubSpot app config, you select “scopes” and OAuth is implied. but in our internal docs we should list “OAuth app w/ refresh token flow” as requirement.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: looping in. couple requirements from security:
1) refresh token must be stored in a managed secrets store (AWS Secrets Manager ok; Vault ok). encrypted at rest + restricted IAM.
2) no tokens in logs. redact + structured logging.
3) token rotation policy: refresh token rotation at least every 90 days OR on employee/offboarding / suspected compromise. confirm hubspot refresh tokens can be revoked + reissued w/o downtime.
4) audit trail: record every token exchange + every API call outcome? at minimum: who/what created/updated CRM objects (service identity), correlation IDs, and an immutable log trail (CloudWatch + retention).

Ethan Park: on (3) — we can revoke the app install and re-install to re-issue refresh token. but that’s disruptive. is there a way to rotate refresh token without uninstall?

David Kim: HubSpot issues new refresh token on re-auth. you can run the OAuth flow again and swap the token. not as disruptive as uninstall; but still “manual” unless we build a token rotation job.

Marcus Lin: so implement “rotate by re-auth” playbook. store token versioned, allow dual token overlap during swap? easiest: write new secret value, deploy picks it up, old one invalidated later.

Priya Natarajan: we should design the secret fetch to support hot reload (polling) or at least quick restart.

Ethan Park: PR currently loads secret at startup. we can add periodic refresh (e.g., every 10m) to pick up secret changes + avoid redeploy.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: periodic refresh is good, but be careful: if HubSpot rate limits token exchange, don’t re-auth too frequently. only refresh access tokens as needed, not refresh tokens.

Caleb Johnson: also note: we saw HubSpot 429s in other contexts. token refresh should be resilient — exponential backoff + jitter, and queue writes.

Logan Wright: +1. plus add metrics: `hubspot_token_refresh_success`, `hubspot_token_refresh_fail`, and `hubspot_auth_401` count. auth 401 spikes are the canary for token rotation issues.

Tyler Benson: re: audit trail — ops needs to be able to answer “why did this account get outreach.” can we include signal id in note body + task subject so it’s traceable?

Ethan Park: yes. note template includes `plg_signal_id`, `signal_type`, `window_start/end`, and `router_version`.

Isabelle Laurent: and please include “source = product signal router” so reps don’t think it’s marketing automation. also if the service updates owner, log it.

Marcus Lin: secrets storage: prefer AWS Secrets Manager with KMS CMK, restricted to service role, no human read. if humans need rotation, use a break-glass role with approval.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: agree. also store HubSpot app client secret in same secret bundle. don’t keep it in CI vars.

David Kim: HubSpot app creds: client_id, client_secret. refresh token is per portal install. we’ll have one portal (Redwood’s) for now. if we add sandbox portal later, we’ll need separate install + separate refresh token.

Ethan Park: ok, so secret structure (conceptually):
- HUBSPOT_CLIENT_ID
- HUBSPOT_CLIENT_SECRET
- HUBSPOT_REFRESH_TOKEN
- HUBSPOT_PORTAL_ID (optional)

Marcus Lin: don’t store as env vars in plaintext. load them at runtime.

Priya Natarajan: what about “who did the API call” visibility in HubSpot? will it show as “App: Redwood Signal Router”?

David Kim: yes, changes will be attributed to the app (good). tasks/notes will show created by integration.

SecurityBot: Reminder: Security review checklist for third-party SaaS integrations: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/security/saas-integrations-checklist (Token storage, logging, rotation, vendor scopes, incident response)

Ethan Park: action items:
1) Ethan: update ENG PR to (a) minimize scopes to list above, (b) add secret hot-reload (poll), (c) add auth-related metrics.
2) David: confirm exact scopes required for notes+tasks association to company (any hidden gotchas) + share HubSpot app config screenshot.
3) Marcus/Aisha: sign off on 90-day refresh token rotation runbook + break-glass access.
4) Tyler/Isabelle: confirm we don’t need contact scopes for MVP + owner mapping approach.

Tyler Benson: marketing ops ok with company-only for MVP. we can add contacts later for lifecycle emails.

Isabelle Laurent: sales ops ok with config mapping for NA pilot. we’ll revisit owner API if we expand teams/regions.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: once you push the changes, tag me + Marcus. include a short “data in/out” section in the ADR: what’s stored, what’s logged, retention.

Marcus Lin: also add a log scrubber test (unit test) to ensure no token strings appear. we’ve been burned by this before.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: will do. will post PR link in this thread when ready.

David Kim: fyi HubSpot OAuth scopes naming sometimes differs in UI vs API docs. I’ll send the canonical list.

Logan Wright: last ask: make sure we set a reasonable access token refresh buffer (refresh when <60s remaining) to avoid concurrent refresh stampede under load.

Ethan Park: yep. will use a singleflight lock around refresh.

Priya Natarajan: thanks all — keep this tight, we want minimal risk before pilot rollout.

title: #eng-releases: 1746810150
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off rollout plan for the new KMS client wrapper stage -> prod. Need owners to sign off on: feature flags, canary criteria, rollback triggers. Targeting first prod canary Monday.

Connor O'Brien: Scope in this rollout: PRs 18421 (wrapper w/ timeouts), 18434 (metrics+tracing), 18449 (circuit breaker + fallback), 18502 (config guardrails), 18515 (error taxonomy fields), 18527 (healthcheck), plus hotfix 18563 already in but we should keep an eye on retry behavior.

Ethan Park: Control-plane side: wrapper is behind `kms.wrapper.enabled` (default false) + dual-read work is separate. For this thread, rollout is just the wrapper + CB policy for config fetch + decrypt paths.

Sean Gallagher: SRE asks: staged rollout by env + region, and by service tier if possible. Also explicit SLO gates: kms latency p95, error rate by category, and overall request 5xx. If we can’t segment by tier, we at least do region+%.

Allison Grant: Observability: dashboards are ready in staging; prod panels exist but need final alert thresholds after canary. Key metrics to watch:
- `redwood.kms.latency_ms` (p50/p95/p99)
- `redwood.kms.errors_total{category=...}` where category in [AccessDenied, NotFound, Throttling, Timeout, InvalidCiphertext, Unknown]
- `redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.open_total`
- `redwood.kms.retry.attempts_total` + `retry_budget_exhausted_total`
- Service-level `5xx_rate` and `decrypt_failures_total`

Logan Wright: Traces: new span name is `kms.call` with tags `kms.provider`, `kms.operation`, `kms.key_id_hash`, `error.category`. Sampling is same as existing dependency spans.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed rollout sequence (pls poke holes):
1) Staging: enable 100% in staging all regions for 24h.
2) Prod canary: enable for 1% traffic in us-east only (hosted) for 2h.
3) Increase to 10% us-east for 4h.
4) Expand to 10% in us-west + eu-west (2h).
5) 50% all prod regions (overnight).
6) 100% all prod regions next business day.

Connor O'Brien: Feature flags/config knobs:
- `kms.wrapper.enabled` (main)
- `kms.circuit_breaker.enabled` (should be true when wrapper enabled; but separate toggle for emergency)
- `kms.retry_budget.override` (should remain false; only for emergency)
- `kms.timeout_ms` and `kms.retry.max_attempts` are now guarded (needs explicit override in prod)

Marcus Lin: Security: fine with staged, but pls ensure rollback doesn’t end up “silent insecure.” Wrapper is integration hardening, no algorithm change, but we must keep “fail closed” on decrypt (no plaintext fallback). Degrade-mode should only affect non-critical paths (metadata fetch, optional features). Confirmed?

Benji Okafor: Confirmed. For decrypt/envelope open: on KMS failures we surface explicit errors, no plaintext path. For control-plane config reads, circuit breaker can serve cached last-known-good for a bounded TTL.

Tomáš Novák: Can we explicitly define rollback triggers for each category? e.g. InvalidCiphertext is highest severity since it can indicate key mismatch/rotation mishap.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add “circuit breaker open rate” as a rollback trigger because it can hide the underlying KMS issue while increasing stale cache risk.

Nadia Rahman: One nuance: `Timeout` and `Throttling` might increase temporarily with the new instrumentation because we’re tagging correctly now. We should compare absolute rates AND correlated 5xx/customer impact.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Rollback should be tied to service impact + sustained elevated dependency failures vs baseline.

Connor O'Brien: Draft canary success criteria (for the canary window, compared to 24h baseline same region):
- No increase in overall `5xx_rate` > +0.2% absolute for >10 min
- `decrypt_failures_total` does not increase > +0.05% of decrypt ops
- `redwood.kms.errors_total{category=InvalidCiphertext}` remains at 0 (or <= 1 event, if it’s known test traffic) — otherwise immediate rollback
- `redwood.kms.latency_ms p95` increase <= +30ms sustained (15 min)
- `Throttling` and `Timeout` categories: ok if they don’t cause retry storms (see below)
- `redwood.kms.retry_budget_exhausted_total` stays flat (no sustained elevation >2x baseline)
- `redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.open_total` <= 1 open/10 min in canary; if it starts flapping, rollback

Allison Grant: I’d tweak CB gate: opens can be ok during a real KMS blip, but we should not see sustained open state >5 min in canary. Also track `half_open` attempts; if we see repeated failures in half-open, that’s a red flag.

Diego Santos: Practical ops: rollback = set `kms.wrapper.enabled=false` via config push (takes ~2 min), no redeploy. Also can disable CB only (`kms.circuit_breaker.enabled=false`) if wrapper is ok but CB is causing unexpected behavior.

Fiona McCarthy: Comms: for prod canary, please post in #incidents only if rollback triggers hit. Otherwise just #eng-releases + #eng-sre for monitoring.

Ethan Park: One more: we should canary in Dedicated too? It uses the same library but different traffic profile. If we can’t segment quickly, at least verify one Dedicated cluster in staging.

Sean Gallagher: Dedicated prod canary is tricky bc blast radius is customer-specific. Suggest: start with Hosted prod canary only, then pick 1 internal Dedicated cluster (dogfood) if we have it. Otherwise schedule Dedicated rollout as a separate change w/ customer comms.

Connor O'Brien: Works. For this thread: Hosted prod first. Dedicated/Private will follow with separate go/no-go and customer schedules.

Marcus Lin: Please document rollback triggers in the change ticket: especially InvalidCiphertext and AccessDenied spikes. AccessDenied often indicates IAM mis-scope in a subset of roles.

Tomáš Novák: +1. Also add a quick query snippet for oncall if we see decrypt failures (log fields from 18515).

Allison Grant: Dropping log query examples to include in the rollout checklist:
- `error.category=InvalidCiphertext service=data-plane` (should be zero)
- `error.category=AccessDenied principal=*` (group by principal/role)
- `error.category=Throttling` (group by region)
- Filter by `kms.operation=Decrypt` vs `GenerateDataKey`

Connor O'Brien: Rollback triggers (hard):
- Any sustained increase in `InvalidCiphertext` (>=2 events in 5 min) -> rollback immediately + page Security + Platform
- Overall 5xx increase > +0.5% absolute for >5 min in canary region -> rollback
- Decrypt failures > 0.1% of decrypt ops for >5 min -> rollback
- Retry amplification: `retry.attempts_total` > 3x baseline AND `timeout` rising -> rollback (avoid storm)

Connor O'Brien: Rollback triggers (soft / investigate):
- `AccessDenied` > 2x baseline -> pause rollout, investigate role/principal; rollback if user-visible errors
- p95 latency increase > +50ms sustained (15 min) -> pause; rollback if correlated w/ request latency SLO misses
- CB opens > 3 times in 10 min -> pause; rollback if open duration > 5 min

Sean Gallagher: Add: if we rollback, we also snapshot dashboard + export trace examples for follow-up (don’t lose the evidence). Owner for that?

Nadia Rahman: I can own “post-rollback evidence pack” for Hosted. I’ll grab:
- 30 min before/after graphs
- top error categories
- top offending services/routes
- 5 sample traces w/ tags

Connor O'Brien: Perfect.

DeployBot: Staging config change queued: set `kms.wrapper.enabled=true`, `kms.circuit_breaker.enabled=true` for env=staging all regions.

Ethan Park: While staging runs, can we confirm config guardrails are effective? (No one can set retry attempts to 10 in prod by accident)

Diego Santos: Yes, 18502 blocks unsafe values unless `kms.retry_budget.override=true` + change reason is included. In prod the override requires oncall role.

Rafael Mendes: For canary windows, can we also set an explicit cap: `max_total_kms_time_ms` per request path? Otherwise retries might eat the whole latency budget.

Benji Okafor: Wrapper enforces a request-scoped budget (defaults to 1s for decrypt path, 500ms for metadata). It will stop retrying once budget is exhausted.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Make sure the canary checklist includes verifying those budgets in the live config.

Connor O'Brien: Checklist draft for Monday canary:
- [ ] Dashboards open (KMS dep + service SLO) + alert routing verified
- [ ] Confirm live config in prod canary region: timeout/budget/retry values
- [ ] Enable `kms.wrapper.enabled=true` for 1% us-east
- [ ] Monitor 30 min, then 2h total
- [ ] If pass, bump to 10% + note in thread
- [ ] Rollback if triggers hit; evidence pack collected

Fiona McCarthy: Please add: confirm incident commander / decision maker. In canary window, who has final call?

Connor O'Brien: Decision maker: Sean (SRE) for service health + Marcus if any crypto correctness concerns (InvalidCiphertext/rotation-like symptoms). Ethan/Benji on standby for code/config.

Marcus Lin: Works. If InvalidCiphertext shows up, assume correctness issue until proven otherwise. Immediate rollback.

Allison Grant: Staging early readout (first 30 min): latency p95 flat, Timeout category down slightly (might be noise), no CB opens. Will keep watching.

DeployBot: Staging rollout complete: `kms.wrapper.enabled=true` (100%).

Sean Gallagher: Nice. Let’s keep staging for 24h. Monday: prod canary 1% us-east at 10:00 PT? Ensure no other high-risk changes in that window.

Connor O'Brien: Scheduled. I’ll post a reminder + link to dashboards Monday morning and call out the rollback triggers again.

Rafael Mendes: Can we add a note: don’t expand to more regions until canary has seen at least one load peak (top-of-hour traffic spike). Otherwise we miss retry behavior.

Connor O'Brien: Good call. We’ll hold at 10% through the 12:00 top-of-hour before expanding.

Sean Gallagher: Approved from SRE side pending clean staging 24h. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: Security approved with the hard rollback trigger language included. :thumbsup:

title: #announcements: 1741036805
preview:
Talia Benson: :loudspeaker: Incident Bot v2 is now **GA** (enabled by default) in `#incidents` and `#eng-oncall`.

Big thanks to SRE + Platform + Telemetry for getting this over the line. The goal is consistent “first 5 minutes” context and lower MTTR.

**What’s new in v2 (high level):**
• **Recent deploys card**: service + version/image SHA, rollout phase (canary vs full), canary health, and links back to the PR/commit + deploy timeline.
• **Customer/tenant-safe config diffs**: feature flags, routing policy, quota/rate-limit, model pins. Includes **redaction** + “why redacted” indicators.
• **Saturation signals**: GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure, scheduler/backpressure indicators, and regional capacity summary.
• **Deep links**: 1-click links to the relevant dashboards/traces (pre-filled time window, service, region).
• **Degraded-mode behavior**: if upstream queries are slow/unhealthy, the bot falls back to cached context + links instead of hanging the thread.

**How to use it**
When an incident thread starts (or you run the bot command), you’ll see context cards posted near the top of the thread.
• If you need more detail on a card, use the interactive actions ("Expand diff", "Open dashboard", etc.).
• If you suspect sensitive info, please **do not paste raw configs**—use the bot diff card and request elevated access via the documented break-glass path.

**Where to file issues / feedback**
• Bugs / missing data / confusing cards: file a Linear ticket under Eng (tag `incident-bot`) or drop a thread in `#help` with a link to the incident thread.
• If you see redaction behaving oddly (too aggressive or too permissive): ping `@Naomi Feldman` / `@Marcus Lin` in-thread and file a ticket.

Rollout note: this is feature-flagged on the backend. If we see regressions (Slack spam, query load, missing context), SRE can roll it back quickly.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: Adding: if a card is empty, please reply in the incident thread with the bot’s error line (it includes the request id). That’s the fastest way for us to trace query timeouts vs permissions vs missing mappings.

Miles Thornton: FYI for on-call: the v2 output is intentionally **summary-first**. Don’t wait for perfect data—use the links to jump into the dashboards while the bot fills in the rest.

Devon Price: If you hit Slack interaction weirdness (buttons spinning / “something went wrong”), try clicking again—actions are idempotent now. If it keeps failing, paste the “interaction_failed” line from the ephemeral message into `#help`.

Allison Grant: Saturation thresholds are standardized now. If the bot flags “HIGH” GPU/queue/KV, please treat it as a triage hint (not a page by itself). The runbook is updated with what-to-do-next for each signal.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy card is driven off the new deploy metadata export. If you see a service with “unknown rollout phase” or missing PR links, tag me + include the service name and approx deploy time window.

Naomi Feldman: Reminder: config diff cards are **tenant-safe** but still considered internal incident data. Please don’t screenshot/share outside approved channels. Redaction + access events are audited.

Marcus Lin: If you believe something sensitive leaked (even if it’s internal), please treat as a security incident and follow the standard reporting path + DM me. We have tests, but we’d rather overreact than miss something.

incident-bot: v2 diagnostics: enabled=true cohort=ga (request_id=diag_7f3a1b)  
Need help? Reply with `request_id` or file: https://linear.app/redwood/team/ENG (tag: incident-bot)

Big thanks to SRE + Platform + Telemetry for getting this over the line. The goal is consistent “first 5 minutes” context and lower MTTR.

**What’s new in v2 (high level):**
• **Recent deploys card**: service + version/image SHA, rollout phase (canary vs full), canary health, and links back to the PR/commit + deploy timeline.
• **Customer/tenant-safe config diffs**: feature flags, routing policy, quota/rate-limit, model pins. Includes **redaction** + “why redacted” indicators.
• **Saturation signals**: GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure, scheduler/backpressure indicators, and regional capacity summary.
• **Deep links**: 1-click links to the relevant dashboards/traces (pre-filled time window, service, region).
• **Degraded-mode behavior**: if upstream queries are slow/unhealthy, the bot falls back to cached context + links instead of hanging the thread.

**How to use it**
When an incident thread starts (or you run the bot command), you’ll see context cards posted near the top of the thread.
• If you need more detail on a card, use the interactive actions ("Expand diff", "Open dashboard", etc.).
• If you suspect sensitive info, please **do not paste raw configs**—use the bot diff card and request elevated access via the documented break-glass path.

**Where to file issues / feedback**
• Bugs / missing data / confusing cards: file a Linear ticket under Eng (tag `incident-bot`) or drop a thread in `#help` with a link to the incident thread.
• If you see redaction behaving oddly (too aggressive or too permissive): ping `@Naomi Feldman` / `@Marcus Lin` in-thread and file a ticket.

Rollout note: this is feature-flagged on the backend. If we see regressions (Slack spam, query load, missing context), SRE can roll it back quickly.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: Adding: if a card is empty, please reply in the incident thread with the bot’s error line (it includes the request id). That’s the fastest way for us to trace query timeouts vs permissions vs missing mappings.

Miles Thornton: FYI for on-call: the v2 output is intentionally **summary-first**. Don’t wait for perfect data—use the links to jump into the dashboards while the bot fills in the rest.

Devon Price: If you hit Slack interaction weirdness (buttons spinning / “something went wrong”), try clicking again—actions are idempotent now. If it keeps failing, paste the “interaction_failed” line from the ephemeral message into `#help`.

Allison Grant: Saturation thresholds are standardized now. If the bot flags “HIGH” GPU/queue/KV, please treat it as a triage hint (not a page by itself). The runbook is updated with what-to-do-next for each signal.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy card is driven off the new deploy metadata export. If you see a service with “unknown rollout phase” or missing PR links, tag me + include the service name and approx deploy time window.

Naomi Feldman: Reminder: config diff cards are **tenant-safe** but still considered internal incident data. Please don’t screenshot/share outside approved channels. Redaction + access events are audited.

Marcus Lin: If you believe something sensitive leaked (even if it’s internal), please treat as a security incident and follow the standard reporting path + DM me. We have tests, but we’d rather overreact than miss something.

incident-bot: v2 diagnostics: enabled=true cohort=ga (request_id=diag_7f3a1b)  
Need help? Reply with `request_id` or file: https://linear.app/redwood/team/ENG (tag: incident-bot)

title: #devex: 1740817442
preview:
Monica Patel: Kicking off a thread to track security review open items for Quickstart Templates (RAG+agents). Want to close these before we call internal beta “ready”. Please drop issues + owner + ETA here.

Marcus Lin: Thx. From the security pass today, top open items:
1) Secrets in logs: a couple places where exceptions may print request headers/resp bodies. Need explicit redaction + test.
2) Sample data: current docs/sample corpus includes “realistic” customer-like content. We need it clearly synthetic + no PII. Also verify license/attribution.
3) Dependency pinning: Python + TS templates still allow floating ranges in a few deps. Need lockfiles + CI to enforce.
4) Redaction processors: OTel collector config needs processors to drop/redact sensitive attrs (headers, prompt content, tool args) by default.

Hanae Suzuki: +1 on (1). In the Python template I saw `logger.exception("request failed", extra={"headers": headers})` in one retry path. If `Authorization` ever gets into that dict we’re toast.
Suggestion: redaction helper that deep-copies dict, strips `authorization`, `x-api-key`, `cookie`, and also anything matching `*_KEY`, `*_TOKEN`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Also check env var naming + docs. We should consistently use `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and never encourage putting keys in `.env` that gets committed.
README should say:
- copy `.env.example` -> `.env` (gitignored)
- use `direnv`/platform secrets manager for deploy
- never paste keys into logs/issues

Dana Lo: TS template: I added structured logging but we’re still doing `console.error(err)` in a couple places. Some errors contain request config which can include headers.
I can swap to a `safeError(err)` serializer that strips `config.headers` and truncates `response.data`.

Allison Grant: On OTel: we should treat traces/metrics/logs as potentially egressing to vendor backends.
Minimum:
- do not emit prompt content by default
- do not emit tool inputs/outputs by default
- include request id + model + status + latency + token counts ok
For local collector compose, add attribute processor rules to delete anything like `http.request.header.authorization` and `http.request.header.cookie`.

Sahana Iyer: Dependency pinning + scanning: for TS we should enforce `pnpm-lock.yaml` (or npm lock) and fail CI if it changes unexpectedly. For Python, `uv lock` or `pip-tools` lockfile.
I can add workflows:
- `dependency-review` (GitHub)
- `pip-audit` / `npm audit` (with allowlist for known false positives)
- `gitleaks` scanning

Wei Chen: Sample data: I can rewrite the sample docs to be clearly synthetic (e.g., Redwood product FAQs) and add a note in the README.
Right now it’s “employee handbook” style which reads too real.

Connor O'Brien: For release hygiene: add `SECURITY.md` + “Reporting vulnerabilities” section, and ensure the template repos ship with `.gitignore` that includes `.env`, `*.pem`, `*.key`, `.otel-collector.yaml` if it contains creds.

Marcus Lin: Specific callouts I saw in code review:
- Python: retry wrapper logs `repr(e)` which includes full URL + query params. If customers put tokens in query params (they shouldn’t, but they do), that gets logged.
- TS: fetch wrapper logs `requestInit` on failure (includes headers).
- Docker compose: example sets `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Bearer ..."` in a commented snippet. That’s risky even commented; people copy/paste.

Monica Patel: Good list. Let’s convert to action items.

Monica Patel: Proposed owners:
(1) Secrets in logs: Dana (TS), Sabrina (Python) w/ review by Hanae + Marcus
(2) Sample data: Wei
(3) Dependency pinning + scanning: Sahana
(4) OTel redaction processors + docs: Allison
(5) SECURITY.md + gitignore hardening: Connor
Everyone ok? ETAs by EOW if possible.

Dana Lo: 👍 I can land TS logging changes tomorrow. Will:
- replace raw `console.error(err)`
- ensure we never log headers/body
- add unit test for redaction (golden snapshot)

Hanae Suzuki: Can I add a small “secrets handling” doc snippet too? People keep using `.env` in production.

Monica Patel: Yes please. Add to the common README troubleshooting section.

Sabrina James: I’ll take Python logging sweep. I’ll grep for `exception(` / `extra={` / `headers` usage and ensure we use a `redact()` helper everywhere.
Also adding a test that asserts logs don’t contain `REDWOOD_API_KEY` when env is set.

Sahana Iyer: For pinning:
- Python: prefer `uv.lock` (fast + deterministic). If we’re not ready for uv, fallback to `requirements.txt` pinned with hashes.
- TS: lockfile + `packageManager` field.
I’ll also add Renovate config to batch updates weekly and require CI green.

Marcus Lin: One more: make sure telemetry tagging (template-origin) is opt-in and doesn’t include user identifiers. I know PM wants adoption metrics, but don’t default to sending extra headers that can trip proxies.

Monica Patel: Yep. Separate but related—telemetry header will be opt-in + documented. We’ll keep it out of this security checklist unless you think it blocks.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: It’s partly security because “unknown headers” sometimes lead people to workaround by dumping more logs. But agree it’s not a hard block as long as opt-in + redaction.

Allison Grant: For collector config, I’ll ship:
- `attributes` processor deleting sensitive attrs
- `transform` processor to drop large payload fields
- examples for local vs prod
Also: add note that users shouldn’t set `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS` in compose files checked into git.

Wei Chen: Sample data rewrite: I’ll replace corpus with:
- synthetic “Redwood Inference docs” snippets
- a few short markdown files in `/sample_docs/`
- add `SAMPLE_DATA.md` explaining it’s synthetic + safe
ETA tomorrow.

Marcus Lin: Great. Blocking criteria for security sign-off:
- no secrets in logs/traces by default
- sample data is clearly synthetic + not derived from customer data
- dependencies pinned + scanning in CI
- collector redaction enabled by default (can be relaxed only with explicit config)

Monica Patel: Cool. I’ll track this against the launch checklist. Please reply in-thread when PRs are up so Marcus can re-review.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll open a PR today for CI + lockfiles and tag @Marcus Lin for a quick pass.

Dana Lo: PR coming shortly. Also removing the commented `Authorization=Bearer ...` snippet from compose and replacing with `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Bearer ${OTEL_BEARER_TOKEN}"` + doc note (and `.env` gitignored).

Hanae Suzuki: Love that. Also consider setting a max log field length (truncate) to avoid accidentally logging tool outputs/HTML/etc.

Sabrina James: +1. I’ll add truncation to the JSON logger formatter (e.g., 2k chars per field) with an allowlist for small fields.

Monica Patel: Thanks all. Next checkpoint: tomorrow EOD I’ll post status + what’s still open.

title: #eng-platform: 1740518300
preview:
Benji Okafor: quick review thread: redaction rules for config diffs (bot v2 + config diff API). goal is customer/tenant-safe diffs in Slack. can we align on allowlist + tests so we don’t leak anything?

Benji Okafor: current draft rules (WIP):
- allowlist specific config paths (feature flags, routing policy, quotas, model pins)
- denylist patterns for keys (token/secret/password/key/etc)
- value redaction if looks like credential (JWT-ish, PEM, long base64)
- default redact unknown keys

Benji Okafor: tagging @Kaitlyn Nguyen @Marcus Lin @Naomi Feldman for security signoff, and @Ethan Park / @Jordan Lee / @Mina Haddad for “what’s actually useful in incident triage”.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 on default-redact unknown keys. if we ever do “best effort detect secrets” without default-redact, we’ll lose.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: question: are we diffing *effective* config or *declared* config? effective config can include merged env/overrides -> higher chance of accidentally pulling creds.

Benji Okafor: good call. current plan is “declared control plane config only” (feature flags / routing policies / quota) not env or runtime vars. we can explicitly exclude env var sources.

Marcus Lin: strong preference: no regex-only approach. do allowlist + schema-based classification. denylist patterns are fine as belt+suspenders.

Marcus Lin: also, Slack is an untrusted sink. treat it as “public-ish internal”. don’t show anything that would be sensitive if screenshotted.

Naomi Feldman: agree. for compliance, the statement should be: “Slack diff output is safe-by-default and does not require elevated clearance.” anything requiring elevated access should be a link gated behind authz + audit.

Mina Haddad: from routing perspective, responders need to see:
- routing policy name/version
- fallback policy enabled/disabled
- regional weights + any recent changes
- pinned model + fallback model
- any load shedding / admission control toggles

Mina Haddad: they do NOT need raw upstream URLs, credentials, header injection rules, or customer identifiers.

Jordan Lee: for policy engine, useful fields are mostly booleans + enums. ex:
- `policy.fallback_mode`
- `policy.max_queue_ms`
- `policy.backpressure.enabled`
- `policy.routing.tier` / `customer_tier`

Jordan Lee: risky fields: anything “expression-like” or templates. we have some rules that include snippets. should redact full contents for those.

Ethan Park: +1. also we should prevent showing full tenant lists. sometimes policies include allow/deny sets by tenant_id.

Benji Okafor: ok so allowlist should be path-based, and for some paths show only summarized version (counts, hash).

Ravi Menon: degraded mode decisions need visibility into *what knobs are set* not the full objects. ex: show “fallback to X region enabled” and “min health threshold = 3/5” rather than full JSON.

Devon Price: from Slack UX side: if we redact heavily, we should be explicit in the card like “(redacted)” + “open in Console (audited)” link.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can we standardize the redaction marker? ex `«REDACTED»` for values and `«REDACTED_KEY»` for keys? helps with automated tests to ensure we didn’t forget.

Benji Okafor: yep. we can also include a “redaction_reason” internally but not show in Slack.

Marcus Lin: careful: don’t include reasons like “matched pattern aws_secret_access_key” in Slack either. keep it simple.

Benji Okafor: proposed allowlist (initial):
```
feature_flags.*
routing.policy_name
routing.policy_version
routing.fallback.enabled
routing.fallback.mode
routing.fallback.regions
routing.weights.*
quota.requests_per_min
quota.tokens_per_min
rate_limit.*  (but only numeric thresholds)
models.primary
models.fallback
models.pin_reason
rollout.phase
rollout.canary.enabled
rollout.canary.percent
```

Benji Okafor: proposed “summary-only” list (show counts + stable hash, not raw contents):
```
routing.tenant_allowlist
routing.tenant_denylist
policy.expressions
policy.templates
```

Ethan Park: looks good. on `rate_limit.*` I’d be specific: allow numeric fields only. sometimes we store “keyed-by” details.

Jordan Lee: +1. we have `rate_limit.key` and `rate_limit.dimensions` -> should redact.

Mina Haddad: also `routing.weights.*` might include region ids only. that’s fine. but if it includes “endpoint” strings, redact.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: tests:
1) unit tests with known-bad keys (`password`, `secret`, `token`, `api_key`, `private_key`) anywhere in path -> always redacted
2) value tests: detect JWT, PEM blocks, long base64, long hex
3) snapshot/golden test of a representative diff payload -> ensure output contains only allowed paths
4) property-based-ish: generate random keys/paths and assert default redacts unless allowlisted

Marcus Lin: +1 and add “negative assertion” tests: if a test config includes a sentinel secret value (e.g. `SUPER_SECRET_DO_NOT_LEAK_123`) it must never appear in output. easy grep.

Naomi Feldman: also ensure audit logging for the underlying fetch is in place, but that’s separate from Slack redaction. Slack can’t be the only control.

Kevin Osei: on audit: if bot hits the config diff endpoint, we log `incident_bot.config_diff.read` with actor, incident id, tenant scope, and whether break-glass was used.

Kevin Osei: redaction happens *before* Slack render, right? (ideally in the config service or diff service so nobody downstream can accidentally log it)

Benji Okafor: yes. plan is redact in the diff service response (shared lib used by config service + incident-bot). Slack renderer only sees redacted fields.

Devon Price: please also ensure we don’t accidentally log the raw diff at debug level in bot. we’ve been bitten by that.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: we’ll add “no raw diff logs” lint/grep in the bot repo too.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: re: break-glass: even with break-glass, Slack should still be redacted. elevated access should mean “can click through to Console view” not “unredacted posted in thread”.

Ethan Park: +1. if someone needs unredacted, they can query in a secured tool, not Slack.

Mina Haddad: can we include a “diff confidence” line? like “partial diff (some fields unavailable)” so responders don’t assume it’s complete.

Benji Okafor: yep. we’ll add flags like `diff_status=complete|partial|unavailable` + error summary (non-sensitive).

Marcus Lin: final ask: document this as an explicit standard: allowlist paths + default redact + tests. and require security review for allowlist expansion.

Benji Okafor: SGTM. action items:
- Benji: update allowlist to be explicit on rate_limit numeric-only + summary-only paths
- Kaitlyn: add sentinel secret tests + jwt/pem/base64 detection tests
- Devon: add bot-side “no raw diff logs” guard
- Marcus/Naomi: quick async signoff once PR is up

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: please add a short note in the card: “Safe view. Some fields intentionally redacted.”

Kevin Osei: and ensure audit event names are stable for SIEM dashboards.

Benji Okafor: cool. I’ll post PR link here when up.

4) property-based-ish: generate random keys/paths and assert default redacts unless allowlisted

Marcus Lin: +1 and add “negative assertion” tests: if a test config includes a sentinel secret value (e.g. `SUPER_SECRET_DO_NOT_LEAK_123`) it must never appear in output. easy grep.

Naomi Feldman: also ensure audit logging for the underlying fetch is in place, but that’s separate from Slack redaction. Slack can’t be the only control.

Kevin Osei: on audit: if bot hits the config diff endpoint, we log `incident_bot.config_diff.read` with actor, incident id, tenant scope, and whether break-glass was used.

Kevin Osei: redaction happens *before* Slack render, right? (ideally in the config service or diff service so nobody downstream can accidentally log it)

Benji Okafor: yes. plan is redact in the diff service response (shared lib used by config service + incident-bot). Slack renderer only sees redacted fields.

Devon Price: please also ensure we don’t accidentally log the raw diff at debug level in bot. we’ve been bitten by that.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: we’ll add “no raw diff logs” lint/grep in the bot repo too.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: re: break-glass: even with break-glass, Slack should still be redacted. elevated access should mean “can click through to Console view” not “unredacted posted in thread”.

Ethan Park: +1. if someone needs unredacted, they can query in a secured tool, not Slack.

Mina Haddad: can we include a “diff confidence” line? like “partial diff (some fields unavailable)” so responders don’t assume it’s complete.

Benji Okafor: yep. we’ll add flags like `diff_status=complete|partial|unavailable` + error summary (non-sensitive).

Marcus Lin: final ask: document this as an explicit standard: allowlist paths + default redact + tests. and require security review for allowlist expansion.

Benji Okafor: SGTM. action items:
- Benji: update allowlist to be explicit on rate_limit numeric-only + summary-only paths
- Kaitlyn: add sentinel secret tests + jwt/pem/base64 detection tests
- Devon: add bot-side “no raw diff logs” guard
- Marcus/Naomi: quick async signoff once PR is up

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: please add a short note in the card: “Safe view. Some fields intentionally redacted.”

Kevin Osei: and ensure audit event names are stable for SIEM dashboards.

Benji Okafor: cool. I’ll post PR link here when up.

title: #eng-platform: 1740172622
preview:
Kaitlyn Nguyen: need to close the loop on bot OAuth/service identity scopes + audit events before we flip config-diff + privileged telemetry on in prod. Can we align today on:
1) exact scopes incident-bot gets
2) what audit events we emit + required fields
3) break-glass flow for Sev0/Sev1

Ethan Park: +1. also want to make sure the control plane gateway enforces scopes consistently across deploy-metadata/config-diff/telemetry endpoints. we’ve got some drift.

Miles Thornton: incident-bot side: we can plumb whatever token/scopes model y’all land on, but we need something stable for v2 beta. main thing: oncall shouldn’t have to ask for perms mid-incident.

Marcus Lin: security req: least privilege by default, and any access to tenant-scoped config/telemetry must be audit logged w/ actor + purpose. “bot did it” isn’t sufficient.

Kevin Osei: + retention + SIEM routing. we need events to show up in audit index with consistent names. please don’t invent 12 variants.

Benji Okafor: for config diff endpoint (`/v1/config/diff` internal), the thing we need is read-only access to diff *after* redaction hooks run. endpoint is tenant-aware and already checks authz, but we need a clean scope for bot.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can do:
- allow `incident-bot` service principal
- require scope `config.diff.read`
- optionally require `incident_context=true` claim so it can’t be used as a general-purpose diff client

Devon Price: question: is this OAuth as in “Slack user installs app and bot inherits user perms” or is it pure service-to-service with a bot identity?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: pure service-to-service for the data plane calls. Slack interaction is user-triggered, but the backend calls are from bot service identity. we’ll include `acting_user` in the audit payload from Slack request context.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side, that’s what we want: bot always works, but we can audit who clicked “Show diff” etc.

Ethan Park: ok so split:
- bot has baseline scopes (deploy context + safe telemetry summaries)
- “expand” actions might require elevated scopes or break-glass?

Marcus Lin: careful: “expand” tends to be where leakage happens. i’d rather keep bot scopes limited and gate any more detailed views behind explicit, audited “break-glass” or per-rotation role.

Miles Thornton: bot UX already has two tiers in the mock:
- card summary (safe)
- button: “View full diff” / “Open telemetry breakdown”
We can make the second path do an extra auth check.

Benji Okafor: config diff output is always redacted (allowlist + secret patterns). but there’s still “sensitive-ish” (customer routing policy, quotas). so yeah still treat as privileged.

Kevin Osei: audit events should capture *what type* of privileged operation happened. ex:
- CONFIG_DIFF_READ
- PRIV_TELEMETRY_QUERY
- BREAK_GLASS_GRANT / BREAK_GLASS_USE
Also include `tenant_id`/`org_id` when applicable, and `incident_id` if present.

Jordan Lee: we can standardize an event schema:
fields:
- event_name
- actor_type (service)
- actor_id (incident-bot)
- acting_user (slack user id/email if we have it)
- reason (e.g., "incident_triage")
- incident_id (from thread/incident command context)
- resource_type (config/metrics)
- resource_id (tenant_id + service + region)
- decision (allow/deny)
- policy_version

Kaitlyn Nguyen: sounds right. for scopes, proposing:
Baseline bot scopes:
- `deploy.metadata.read` (recent deploys card)
- `telemetry.saturation.read` (gpu util/queue depth/kv cache pressure *aggregated only*)
- `slo.burn.read` (error budget burn link gen)
Privileged scopes (only when user clicks expand AND they’re in allowed group / break-glass):
- `config.diff.read`
- `telemetry.tenant_query.read` (tenant-aware query primitives)

Ethan Park: does `telemetry.saturation.read` hit the same query service as tenant-aware queries? want to avoid “baseline scope still lets you do arbitrary queries”

Logan Wright (via Miles, pasted): baseline saturation can be a separate endpoint with fixed query templates + hard-coded label filters. tenant_query is the flexible one. keep them separate.

Devon Price: implementation detail: Slack action -> bot backend -> call authz service “can user X request privileged operation Y for incident Z?”
If allowed, bot uses *same* service token but with a different scope? or do we mint a short-lived token with elevated scope?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: mint short-lived token with elevated scope via internal auth service. scope bound + TTL 5m. include `acting_user` + `incident_id` claims.

Marcus Lin: +1 short-lived. also require that the privileged token includes `purpose=incident` and `channel=#incidents|#eng-oncall` (or at least source context). if someone replays it elsewhere, it should fail.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can enforce claim `purpose=incident_triage` and `incident_id` present for `config.diff.read` and `telemetry.tenant_query.read`.

Benji Okafor: for break-glass: do we pre-provision “OncallIncidentBotPrivileged” group membership, or do we have an explicit “request break-glass” button?

Rafael Mendes: for the beta, simplest is group-based: SRE oncall rotation + platform oncall have it. Longer term, we can do “request break-glass” with approval trail.

Kevin Osei: group-based still needs audit. every privileged read emits event; monthly review pulls from SIEM.

Ethan Park: and what about bot *itself* calling privileged endpoints in degraded mode? e.g., if auth service is down.

Miles Thornton: if authz is down we should fail closed for privileged (show link + “permissions check unavailable”), but still show baseline cards. no “silent allow” during outage.

Marcus Lin: yes. fail closed for privileged.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok, action items:
- Kaitlyn: write up final scopes list + claims requirements and drop in ADR comment (today)
- Jordan: update policy engine rules for `config.diff.read` + `telemetry.tenant_query.read` with required claims + deny-by-default (tomorrow)
- Benji: ensure config diff endpoint emits audit event with schema above and includes redaction version/hash (this week)
- Kevin: confirm event names + SIEM routing + retention reqs, share list of required fields (today)
- Devon/Miles: bot path mints 5m privileged token on “expand” + passes acting_user/incident_id + handles fail-closed (this week)

Kevin Osei: for event naming, propose:
- `audit.incident_bot.config_diff.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.telemetry.tenant_query.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.break_glass.token_issued`
Keep in lower_snake or dotted, but consistent.

Marcus Lin: + include `redaction_profile` and `result_count` (e.g., diff lines) to help spot weird usage patterns.

Devon Price: ack. for acting_user, we can map Slack user -> email via SCIM. if missing, log slack user id.

Ethan Park: please also log `service` + `region` on telemetry queries. otherwise it’s useless in reviews.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: got it. i’ll send a short summary in-thread once the doc is updated.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: once scopes are final, we’ll update the bot env + start staging validation.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: will post policy diff PR link here when ready.

title: #eng-platform: 1740112830
preview:
Jordan Lee: Kicking off a thread on rollout policy guardrail edge cases. We keep running into ambiguities in reviews: (1) step size vs approval gates, (2) soak time overrides, (3) admin roles / who can bypass. Want to align before we lock policy engine semantics for GA.

Paula Mendes: +1. This is the stuff customers will hit immediately. For Hosted GA I want the Console to have predictable errors + a path forward (not just “denied”).

Benji Okafor: Starting list of edge cases:
- If max_step_percent=10 and you have approvals required at >=25%, can you jump 10->25 (15pt jump) with approval? or still blocked?
- If min_soak=30m, can SRE/owner force-progress early (like 5m) if metrics are clean?
- Who is “admin”? tenant_admin vs org_owner vs rollout_approver.

Ethan Park: Also: what happens when policy changes mid-rollout. E.g. admin tightens max_step from 25->5 while rollout is paused at 10%.

Jordan Lee: Yup. My current leaning: approvals are additive, not a bypass. I.e. approval gate doesn’t let you exceed max step. Approval just authorizes actions that are otherwise allowed.

Paula Mendes: That’s the safest, but then UX needs to explain why “approved” still can’t go 10->25.

Chloe Martin: From Console POV we can handle it if policy engine returns structured reason: `max_step_exceeded` vs `approval_required`. We’ll show both if needed.

Benji Okafor: Concrete example:
- baseline at 0%
- step schedule: 1, 5, 25, 50, 100
- policy: max_step=10, approvals_required_at=[25,50,100]
If approvals don’t bypass, schedule is invalid unless user inserts intermediate steps (15, 25). That’s OK, but wizard should catch it on “Review”.

Jordan Lee: Agree. We should validate schedule at creation time (and also at execution if step edited).

Hannah Rivera: Orchestrator can enforce at “advance step” time only. Creation-time validation is nicer for UX but we still need runtime enforcement for API callers.

Paula Mendes: Make it both. Wizard preview should flag “this schedule can’t run under current policy”. API should return same code.

Sean Gallagher: On soak time: strong preference that min soak is not bypassable by tenant users. If we let people skip soak, they’ll do it under pressure and then blame us when it goes sideways.

Rafael Mendes: +1. For reliability we want soak time to mean something. If we allow overrides, it should be a separate “break glass” role that’s very explicit + audited.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: On roles: today we have tenant_admin and a few internal roles. The new RBAC PR introduces `rollout_approver`. We should be careful not to implicitly treat tenant_admin as “can do everything” because enterprise customers expect separation of duties.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: bypasses should be extremely limited. If we introduce any “override” mechanism, it needs:
1) explicit permission (separate from tenant_admin)
2) audit event with reason string
3) customer-visible activity log entry

Jordan Lee: So a possible model:
- max_step_percent: hard limit, never bypassed
- min_soak_duration: hard limit for customers; internal SRE can bypass only with break-glass
- approval gates: require `rollout_approver` to proceed past configured thresholds; does not relax other constraints

Paula Mendes: I’m good with that for GA, but can we define what “thresholds” mean? Is it “when canary_percent >= X” or “when you increase by >= X”? Customers asked “approval at 50%” meaning “before you go to 50% or higher”.

Benji Okafor: I assumed “target percent”. So going to 50 triggers approval. Going 49->51 triggers too.

Jordan Lee: Same. Gate = approval required for any action that results in `target_canary_percent >= gate_percent`.

Ethan Park: Edge: rollback action. If you roll back from 50 to 0, do you need approval? (some orgs require approval for any traffic changes, including rollback)

Paula Mendes: For GA I think rollback should be always allowed to anyone who can manage rollouts. That’s safety.

Marcus Lin: Usually “emergency changes” are permitted. But we can still require audit (who rolled back). I’d avoid requiring approval to roll back.

Sean Gallagher: Please don’t gate rollback. We need fast mitigation.

Jordan Lee: Ok: rollback exempt from approval gates. Still subject to permission (operator role) and audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Then we need two perms:
- `rollouts.execute` (create/start/pause/advance/rollback)
- `rollouts.approve` (approve gated steps)
Tenant_admin can be configured to have both but not implied.

Chloe Martin: Wizard copy: when approval is required, we can show “Pending approval by Rollout Approver” + list eligible roles. Also show link to docs.

Benji Okafor: Back to policy changes mid-rollout:
If max_step lowers, we don’t want already-executed steps to become “invalid”. But next action should evaluate against current policy.

Jordan Lee: Yup. Policy evaluated at action time. Rollout object stores what happened; policy just decides if the *next* action is allowed.

Ethan Park: That implies a weird case: rollout created with 25% step, then admin tightens max_step to 10 before hitting it. Now it’s blocked unless user edits schedule.

Paula Mendes: That’s acceptable, but Console should explain “policy changed since creation” and offer “Edit schedule” CTA.

Hannah Rivera: We’ll need a dedicated error code for that. Otherwise it looks like a random failure.

Jordan Lee: Proposed error taxonomy (for Console + API):
- `ROLL_POLICY_MAX_STEP_EXCEEDED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_MIN_SOAK_NOT_MET`
- `ROLL_POLICY_APPROVAL_REQUIRED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_ACTION_NOT_PERMITTED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_POLICY_CHANGED_BLOCKING`

Benji Okafor: Another edge: min soak per step vs total soak. I think it’s per step (time since last traffic change), right?

Sean Gallagher: Per step. We care that each increase gets a soak. Otherwise people do 1% then wait 30m then instantly go 5->100.

Jordan Lee: Per step: `now - last_traffic_shift_ts >= min_soak_duration`.

Rafael Mendes: Also: if auto-pause triggered (SLO breach), do we enforce a new min soak after resume? I’d say yes (since resume may include a step increase).

Jordan Lee: Resume itself shouldn’t require soak; only advancing traffic does.

Paula Mendes: Can we clarify “pause” vs “stop” semantics in policy? E.g. auto-stop might force rollback. That shouldn’t be blocked by “min soak not met”.

Jordan Lee: Good call: system-initiated safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass soak + approval checks but still log as system actor.

Marcus Lin: Make sure audit distinguishes:
- actor_type: user / system / internal
- if internal override, include ticket/incident ref if available

Chloe Martin: We can display as “System (Auto-stop) paused rollout due to p95 latency threshold breach.” That’s consistent with timeline designs.

Benji Okafor: One more: “approval required at 25%” and max_step=10. User at 20% wants to go to 25% (5pt jump). Needs approval, but the approval action itself—do we require approval before setting target=25, or can they set it and it stays pending?

Jordan Lee: I’d prefer two-step:
1) proposer sets next target (pending)
2) approver approves and then action executes
That avoids the weirdness of “approval required” blocking even creating the pending request.

Paula Mendes: +1. Console flow already has “Submit for approval”.

Hannah Rivera: Orchestrator will need a “pending_approval” state for step transitions. We already have a draft state machine; I’ll make sure it covers this.

Sean Gallagher: If it’s pending approval, traffic shouldn’t change until approved (obvious, but calling it out).

Jordan Lee: Summarizing decisions (unless objections):
- Approval gates do NOT bypass max step or min soak.
- max_step_percent: hard limit, no bypass.
- min_soak_duration: hard limit for customers; only internal break-glass can bypass (not in GA UI).
- Approval required when resulting target_canary_percent >= gate threshold.
- Rollback is never approval-gated; always allowed for operators.
- Policy evaluated at action time; policy changes can block future steps.
- System safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass approval/soak but are audited as system actor.
- Implement pending approval step transition.

Paula Mendes: Works for me. I’ll add this to PM-1851 notes + call out in docs as “Approvals don’t override safety limits”.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update policy bundle examples + add unit tests for 10->25 case + policy-change-mid-rollout case.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll double-check RBAC mapping so tenant_admin isn’t implicitly granted `rollouts.approve`.

Marcus Lin: Please include a “reason” field in any override audit event even if we don’t surface override in GA. We’ll need it later.

Jordan Lee: Done. I’ll capture as an ADR comment in ENG-9146 and link this thread.
